From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Island in the North Atlantic Ocean

This article is about the island. For the sovereign state, see [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"). For the part of the United Kingdom, see [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). For other uses, see [Ireland (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ireland_(disambiguation) "Ireland (disambiguation)").

**Ireland**[[b]](#cite_note-11) is an island in the North [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), in [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe"). Geopolitically, the island is divided between the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") (officially [named Ireland](/wiki/Names_of_the_Irish_state "Names of the Irish state") – a sovereign state covering five-sixths of the island) and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") (part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") – covering the remaining sixth). It is separated from [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") to its east by the [North Channel](/wiki/North_Channel_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "North Channel (Great Britain and Ireland)"), the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), and [St George's Channel](/wiki/St_George%27s_Channel "St George's Channel"). Ireland is the [second-largest island](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_British_Isles "List of islands of the British Isles") of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), the [third-largest](/wiki/List_of_European_islands_by_area "List of European islands by area") in Europe, and the [twentieth-largest](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_area "List of islands by area") in the world.[[10]](#cite_note-unep-12) As of 2022, the [population of the entire island](/wiki/Irish_population_analysis "Irish population analysis") is just over 7 million, with 5.1 million in the Republic of Ireland and 1.9 million in Northern Ireland, ranking it the [second-most populous island in Europe](/wiki/List_of_European_islands_by_population "List of European islands by population") after Great Britain.[[11]](#cite_note-2022population-13)

The [geography of Ireland](/wiki/Geography_of_Ireland "Geography of Ireland") comprises relatively low-lying mountains surrounding a central plain, with [several navigable rivers](/wiki/Rivers_of_Ireland "Rivers of Ireland") extending inland. Much of Ireland was woodland until the end of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Today, woodland makes up about 10% of the island, compared with a European average of over 33%,[[12]](#cite_note-forest-14) with most of it being non-native conifer plantations.[[13]](#cite_note-forest2-15)[[14]](#cite_note-forest3-16) Its lush vegetation is a product of [its mild climate](/wiki/Climate_of_Ireland "Climate of Ireland"), which is influenced by the Atlantic Ocean and thus very moderate,[[15]](#cite_note-17) and winters are milder than expected for such a northerly area, although summers are cooler than those in continental Europe. Rainfall and cloud cover are abundant.

[Gaelic Ireland](/wiki/Gaelic_Ireland "Gaelic Ireland") had emerged by the 1st century AD. The island was [Christianised](/wiki/Christianised "Christianised") from the 5th century onwards. During this period Ireland was divided amongst petty kings, who in turn served under the kings of the [traditional provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland") (*Cúige*; lit. 'fifth') vying for dominance and the title of [High King of Ireland](/wiki/High_King_of_Ireland "High King of Ireland"). Between the late 8th and early 11th centuries, [Viking raids and settlement](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age") took place culminating in the [Battle of Clontarf](/wiki/Battle_of_Clontarf "Battle of Clontarf") on 23 April 1014 which resulted in the end of Viking power in Ireland. Following the 12th-century [Anglo-Norman invasion](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_invasion_of_Ireland "Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland"), [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") claimed sovereignty. However, English rule did not extend over the whole island until the 16th–17th century [Tudor conquest](/wiki/Tudor_conquest_of_Ireland "Tudor conquest of Ireland"), which led to [colonisation by settlers from Britain](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland"). In the 1690s, a system of [Protestant English rule](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy") was designed to materially disadvantage the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") majority and [Protestant dissenters](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters"), and was extended during the 18th century. With the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") in 1801, Ireland became [a part of](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). The [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") of the 1840s saw the population fall by over 20%, through death and emigration. A [war of independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") in the early 20th century was followed by the [partition of the island](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland"), leading to the creation of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), which became increasingly sovereign over the following decades until it declared a republic in 1948 (Republic of Ireland Act, 1948) and Northern Ireland, which remained a part of the United Kingdom. Northern Ireland saw much [civil unrest from the late 1960s until the 1990s](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles"). This subsided following the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") in 1998. In 1973, both the Republic of Ireland and the United Kingdom, with Northern Ireland as part of it, joined the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"). Following a referendum vote in 2016, the United Kingdom, Northern Ireland included, left the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) in 2020. Northern Ireland was granted a limited special status and allowed to operate within the EU single market for goods without being in the European Union.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

[Irish culture](/wiki/Irish_culture "Irish culture") has had a significant influence on other cultures, especially in the field of [literature](/wiki/Irish_literature "Irish literature"). Alongside mainstream [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), a strong indigenous culture exists, as expressed through [Gaelic games](/wiki/Gaelic_games "Gaelic games"), [Irish music](/wiki/Irish_music "Irish music"), [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"), and [Irish dance](/wiki/Irish_dance "Irish dance"). The island's culture shares many features with that of Great Britain, including the English language, and sports such as association football, rugby, horse racing, golf, and boxing.

## Name

The names *Ireland* and *Éire* both derive from [Old Irish](/wiki/Old_Irish "Old Irish") *[Ériu](/wiki/%C3%89riu "Ériu")*, which in turn comes from [Proto-Celtic](/wiki/Proto-Celtic "Proto-Celtic") *[ɸīweriyū](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Celtic/%C9%B8%C4%ABweriy%C5%AB "wiktionary:Reconstruction:Proto-Celtic/ɸīweriyū")* meaning "fertile soil". The [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") name for the island of Ireland, *[Ivernia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Ivernia#Latin "wiktionary:Ivernia")*, also comes from this same root,[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20) and it remains a popular, poetic synonym for the island (usually respelt "*[Hibernia](/wiki/Hibernia "Hibernia")*", [under the influence](/wiki/Morphological_reanalysis "Morphological reanalysis") of *[hibernum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hibernum#Latin "wiktionary:hibernum")*, the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") word for "winter").

## History

Main article: [History of Ireland](/wiki/History_of_Ireland "History of Ireland")

### Prehistoric Ireland

Main article: [Prehistoric Ireland](/wiki/Prehistoric_Ireland "Prehistoric Ireland")

During the [last glacial period](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period"), and until about 16,000 BC, much of Ireland was periodically covered in ice.[[19]](#cite_note-National_Museum-21) The [relative sea level](/wiki/Relative_sea_level "Relative sea level") was less than 50 m lower resulting in an [ice bridge](/wiki/Ice_bridge "Ice bridge") (but not a [land bridge](/wiki/Land_bridge "Land bridge")) forming between Ireland and Great Britain.[[20]](#cite_note-Marine_Geology-22) By 14,000 BC this ice bridge existed only between Northern Ireland and Scotland and by 12,000 BC Ireland was completely separated from Great Britain.[[21]](#cite_note-drowning-23) Later, around 6,100 BC, Great Britain became separated from continental Europe.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Until recently, the earliest evidence of human activity in Ireland was dated at 12,500 years ago, demonstrated by a butchered bear bone found in a cave in [County Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare").[[23]](#cite_note-BBC2016-03-21-25) In 2021, a reindeer bone with chop marks, which had been found in Castlepook Cave near [Doneraile](/wiki/Doneraile "Doneraile"), County Cork in the early twentieth century during [Richard J. Ussher](/wiki/Richard_J._Ussher "Richard J. Ussher")'s excavations, was dated to 33,000 years ago by Dr Ruth Carden, a [paleozoologist](/wiki/Paleozoologist "Paleozoologist") from the School of Archaeology in [University College Dublin](/wiki/University_College_Dublin "University College Dublin"), pushing back the earliest signs of human activity by 20,000 years.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

By about 8,000 BC, more sustained occupation of the island has been shown, with evidence for [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") communities around the island.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

Some time before 4,000 BC, [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe") [settlers](/wiki/Early_European_Farmers "Early European Farmers") introduced cereal [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar"), domesticated animals such as cattle and sheep, built large timber buildings, and stone monuments.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[19]](#cite_note-National_Museum-21) The earliest evidence for farming in Ireland or Great Britain is from [Ferriter's Cove](/wiki/Ferriter%27s_Cove "Ferriter's Cove"), [County Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry"), where a flint knife, cattle bones and a sheep's tooth were [carbon-dated](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") to c. 4,350 BC.[[27]](#cite_note-Ireland's_DNA-29) Field systems were developed in different parts of Ireland, including at the *[Céide Fields](/wiki/C%C3%A9ide_Fields "Céide Fields")*, that has been preserved beneath a blanket of peat in present-day [Tyrawley](/wiki/Tyrawley "Tyrawley") in northwest Mayo. An extensive [field system](/wiki/Field_system "Field system"), arguably the oldest in the world,[[28]](#cite_note-30) consisted of small divisions separated by [dry-stone walls](/wiki/Dry-stone_wall "Dry-stone wall"). The fields were farmed for several centuries between 3,500 BC and 3,000 BC. [Wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") were the principal crops.[[19]](#cite_note-National_Museum-21)

The [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") began around 2,500 BC, with technology changing people's everyday lives during this period through innovations such as the wheel, harnessing [oxen](/wiki/Oxen "Oxen"), [weaving textiles](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving"), brewing [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") and [metalworking](/wiki/Metalworking "Metalworking"),[[19]](#cite_note-National_Museum-21) which produced new weapons and tools, along with fine gold decoration and jewellery, such as [brooches](/wiki/Brooch "Brooch") and [torcs](/wiki/Torc "Torc").

### Celts

How and when the island became Celtic has been debated for close to a century, with the migrations of the [Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts") being one of the more enduring themes of archaeological and linguistic studies. The most recent genetic research strongly associates the spread of [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") (including [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages")) through Western Europe with a people bringing a composite [Bell Beaker culture](/wiki/Bell_Beaker_culture "Bell Beaker culture"), with its arrival in Britain and Ireland dated to around the middle of the third millennium BC.[[29]](#cite_note-Reich_2018_115-31) According to [John T. Koch](/wiki/John_T._Koch "John T. Koch") and others, Ireland in the Late Bronze Age was part of a maritime trading culture called the [Atlantic Bronze Age](/wiki/Atlantic_Bronze_Age "Atlantic Bronze Age") that also included Britain, western France and Iberia, and that this is where Celtic languages developed.[[30]](#cite_note-Koch-32)[[31]](#cite_note-Koch2009-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35) This contrasts with the traditional view that their origin lies in mainland Europe with the [Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture").[[34]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Uragh_Stone_Circle.jpg/250px-Uragh_Stone_Circle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uragh_Stone_Circle.jpg)

[The Uragh Stone Circle](/wiki/Uragh_Stone_Circle "Uragh Stone Circle"), a [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") [stone circle](/wiki/Stone_circle "Stone circle") in [Tuosist](/wiki/Tuosist "Tuosist"), close to Gleninchaquin Park, [County Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry")

The long-standing traditional view is that the Celtic language, [Ogham](/wiki/Ogham "Ogham") script and culture were brought to Ireland by waves of invading or migrating Celts from mainland Europe. This theory draws on the *[Lebor Gabála Érenn](/wiki/Lebor_Gab%C3%A1la_%C3%89renn "Lebor Gabála Érenn")*, a medieval Christian pseudo-history of Ireland, along with the presence of Celtic culture, language and artefacts found in Ireland such as Celtic bronze spears, shields, torcs and other finely crafted Celtic associated possessions. The theory holds that there were four separate Celtic invasions of Ireland. The [Priteni](/wiki/Priteni "Priteni") were said to be the first, followed by the [Belgae](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae") from northern [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") and Britain. Later, [Laighin](/wiki/Laigin "Laigin") tribes from [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica") were said to have invaded Ireland and Britain more or less simultaneously. Lastly, the [Milesians](/wiki/Milesians_(Irish) "Milesians (Irish)") ([Gaels](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels")) were said to have reached Ireland from either northern Iberia or southern Gaul.[[35]](#cite_note-37) It was claimed that a second wave named the Euerni, belonging to the Belgae people of northern Gaul, began arriving about the 6th century BC. They were said to have given their name to the island.[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The theory was advanced in part because of the lack of archaeological evidence for large-scale Celtic immigration, though it is accepted that such movements are notoriously difficult to identify. Historical linguists are skeptical that this method alone could account for the absorption of Celtic language, with some saying that an assumed processual view of Celtic linguistic formation is 'an especially hazardous exercise'.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) Genetic lineage investigation into the area of Celtic migration to Ireland has led to findings that showed no significant differences in [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") between Ireland and large areas of continental Europe, in contrast to parts of the Y-chromosome pattern. When taking both into account, a study concluded that modern Celtic speakers in Ireland could be thought of as European "Atlantic Celts" showing a shared ancestry throughout the Atlantic zone from northern Iberia to western Scandinavia rather than substantially central European.[[40]](#cite_note-42)
In 2012, research showed that the occurrence of genetic markers for the earliest farmers was almost eliminated by Beaker-culture immigrants: they carried what was then a new Y-chromosome R1b marker, believed to have originated in Iberia about 2,500 BC. The prevalence amongst modern Irish men of this mutation is a remarkable 84%, the highest in the world, and closely matched in other populations along the Atlantic fringes down to Spain. A similar genetic replacement happened with lineages in mitochondrial DNA.[[27]](#cite_note-Ireland's_DNA-29)[[41]](#cite_note-43) This conclusion is supported by recent research carried out by the geneticist [David Reich](/wiki/David_Reich_(geneticist) "David Reich (geneticist)"), who says: "British and Irish skeletons from the Bronze Age that followed the Beaker period had at most 10 per cent ancestry from the first farmers of these islands, with other 90 per cent from people like those associated with the Bell Beaker culture in the Netherlands." He suggests that it was Beaker users who introduced an Indo-European language, represented here by Celtic (i.e. a new language and culture introduced directly by migration and genetic replacement).[[29]](#cite_note-Reich_2018_115-31)

### Late antiquity and early medieval times

Main article: [History of Ireland (800–1169)](/wiki/History_of_Ireland_(800%E2%80%931169) "History of Ireland (800–1169)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Dalriada.png/250px-Dalriada.png)](/wiki/File:Dalriada.png)

The [Scoti](/wiki/Scoti "Scoti") were [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels")-speaking people from Ireland who settled in western Scotland in the 6th century or before.

The earliest written records of Ireland come from classical [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world") geographers. [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in his *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* refers to Ireland as *Mikra Brettania* ("Little Britain"), in contrast to the larger island which he called *Megale Brettania* ("Great Britain").[[42]](#cite_note-44) In [his map of Ireland](/wiki/Ptolemy%27s_map_of_Ireland "Ptolemy's map of Ireland") in his later work, *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")*, Ptolemy refers to Ireland as *[Iouernia](/wiki/Hibernia "Hibernia")* and to Great Britain as *Albion*. These names were likely to have been the local names for the islands at the time. The earlier names, [in contrast](/wiki/Exonym_and_endonym "Exonym and endonym"), were likely to have been coined before direct contact with local peoples was made.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

The [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") referred to Ireland by this name too in its [Latinised](/wiki/Latinisation_of_names "Latinisation of names") form, *Hibernia*, or [Scotia](/wiki/Scotia "Scotia").[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47) Ptolemy records 16 nations inhabiting every part of Ireland in 100 AD.[[46]](#cite_note-48) The relationship between the Roman Empire and the kingdoms of ancient Ireland is unclear. However, finds of Roman coins have been made, for example at the Iron Age settlement of Freestone Hill near [Gowran](/wiki/Gowran "Gowran") and [Newgrange](/wiki/Newgrange "Newgrange").[[47]](#cite_note-49)

Ireland continued as a patchwork of rival kingdoms; however, beginning in the 7th century, a concept of national kingship gradually became articulated through the concept of a [High King of Ireland](/wiki/High_King_of_Ireland "High King of Ireland"). [Medieval Irish literature](/wiki/Medieval_Irish_literature "Medieval Irish literature") portrays an almost unbroken sequence of high kings stretching back thousands of years, but some modern historians believe the scheme was constructed in the 8th century to justify the status of powerful political groupings by projecting the origins of their rule into the remote past.[[48]](#cite_note-DOC-50)

All of the Irish kingdoms had their own kings but were nominally subject to the high king. The high king was drawn from the ranks of the provincial kings and ruled also the royal [kingdom of Meath](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Meath "Kingdom of Meath"), with a ceremonial capital at the [Hill of Tara](/wiki/Hill_of_Tara "Hill of Tara"). The concept did not become a political reality until the [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age#Ireland "Viking Age") and even then was not a consistent one.[[49]](#cite_note-51) Ireland did have a culturally unifying rule of law: the early written judicial system, the [Brehon Laws](/wiki/Brehon_Laws "Brehon Laws"), administered by a professional class of jurists known as the *brehons*.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

*[The Chronicle of Ireland](/wiki/The_Chronicle_of_Ireland "The Chronicle of Ireland")* records that in 431, Bishop [Palladius](/wiki/Palladius_(bishop_of_Ireland) "Palladius (bishop of Ireland)") arrived in Ireland on a mission from [Pope Celestine I](/wiki/Pope_Celestine_I "Pope Celestine I") to minister to the Irish "already believing in Christ".[[51]](#cite_note-53) The same chronicle records that [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick"), Ireland's best known [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saints_of_places "Patron saints of places"), arrived the following year. There is continued debate over the missions of Palladius and Patrick, but the consensus is that they both took place[[52]](#cite_note-54) and that the older [druid](/wiki/Druid "Druid") tradition collapsed in the face of Christianity.[[53]](#cite_note-cah-55) Irish Christian scholars excelled in the study of Latin and Greek learning and Christian theology. In the monastic culture that followed the Christianisation of Ireland, Latin and Greek learning was preserved in Ireland during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") in contrast to elsewhere in Western Europe, where the [Dark Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") followed the [Fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire").[[53]](#cite_note-cah-55)[[54]](#cite_note-Eer-56)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg/250px-KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)](/wiki/File:KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)

A folio of the [Book of Kells](/wiki/Book_of_Kells "Book of Kells") showing Christ enthroned

The arts of [manuscript illumination](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript"), metalworking and sculpture flourished and produced treasures such as the *[Book of Kells](/wiki/Book_of_Kells "Book of Kells")*, ornate jewellery and the many carved stone crosses[[55]](#cite_note-57) that still dot the island today. A mission founded in 563 on [Iona](/wiki/Iona "Iona") by the Irish monk Saint [Columba](/wiki/Columba "Columba") began [a tradition of Irish missionary](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission") work that spread [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") and learning to Scotland, England and the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") on continental Europe after the fall of Rome.[[56]](#cite_note-58) These missions continued until the [late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"), establishing monasteries and centres of learning, producing scholars such as [Sedulius Scottus](/wiki/Sedulius_Scottus "Sedulius Scottus") and [Johannes Eriugena](/wiki/Johannes_Eriugena "Johannes Eriugena") and exerting much influence in Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From the 9th century, waves of [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") raiders plundered Irish monasteries and towns.[[57]](#cite_note-59) These raids added to a pattern of raiding and [endemic warfare](/wiki/Endemic_warfare "Endemic warfare") that was already deep-seated in Ireland. The Vikings were involved in establishing most of the major coastal settlements in Ireland: [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick"), [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"), [Wexford](/wiki/Wexford "Wexford"), [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford "Waterford"), as well as other smaller settlements.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

### Norman and English invasions

Main articles: [Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_invasion_of_Ireland "Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland"), [History of Ireland (1169–1536)](/wiki/History_of_Ireland_(1169%E2%80%931536) "History of Ireland (1169–1536)"), and [Tudor conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Tudor_conquest_of_Ireland "Tudor conquest of Ireland")

See also: [Bruce campaign in Ireland](/wiki/Bruce_campaign_in_Ireland "Bruce campaign in Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Trim_Castle_6.jpg/250px-Trim_Castle_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trim_Castle_6.jpg)

Remains of the 12th-century [Trim Castle](/wiki/Trim_Castle "Trim Castle") in [County Meath](/wiki/County_Meath "County Meath"), the largest [Norman castle](/wiki/Norman_architecture "Norman architecture") in Ireland

On 1 May 1169, an expedition of [Cambro-Norman](/wiki/Cambro-Norman "Cambro-Norman") knights, with an army of about 600 men, landed at [Bannow Strand](/wiki/Bannow "Bannow") in present-day [County Wexford](/wiki/County_Wexford "County Wexford"). It was led by [Richard de Clare](/wiki/Richard_de_Clare,_2nd_Earl_of_Pembroke "Richard de Clare, 2nd Earl of Pembroke"), known as 'Strongbow' owing to his prowess as an archer.[[59]](#cite_note-61) The invasion, which coincided with a period of renewed Norman expansion, was at the invitation of [Dermot Mac Murrough](/wiki/Diarmait_Mac_Murchada "Diarmait Mac Murchada"), [King of Leinster](/wiki/Kings_of_Leinster "Kings of Leinster").[[60]](#cite_note-62)

In 1166, Mac Murrough had fled to [Anjou](/wiki/County_of_Anjou "County of Anjou"), France, following a war involving [Tighearnán Ua Ruairc](/wiki/Tighearn%C3%A1n_Ua_Ruairc "Tighearnán Ua Ruairc"), of [Breifne](/wiki/Breifne "Breifne"), and sought the assistance of the [Angevin](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire") King [Henry II](/wiki/King_Henry_II_of_England "King Henry II of England"), in recapturing his kingdom. In 1171, Henry arrived in Ireland in order to review the general progress of the expedition. He wanted to re-exert royal authority over the invasion which was expanding beyond his control. Henry successfully re-imposed his authority over Strongbow and the Cambro-Norman warlords and persuaded many of the Irish kings to accept him as their overlord, an arrangement confirmed in the 1175 [Treaty of Windsor](/wiki/Treaty_of_Windsor_1175 "Treaty of Windsor 1175").

The invasion was legitimised by reference to provisions of the alleged [Papal Bull](/wiki/Papal_Bull "Papal Bull") *[Laudabiliter](/wiki/Laudabiliter "Laudabiliter")*, issued by an Englishman, [Adrian IV](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_IV "Pope Adrian IV"), in 1155. The document apparently encouraged Henry to take control in Ireland in order to oversee the financial and administrative reorganisation of the [Irish Church](/wiki/Celtic_Church "Celtic Church") and its integration into the Roman Church system.[[61]](#cite_note-Curtis_2002_49-63) Some restructuring had already begun at the ecclesiastical level following the [Synod of Kells](/wiki/Synod_of_Kells "Synod of Kells") in 1152.[[62]](#cite_note-64) There has been significant controversy regarding the authenticity of *Laudabiliter*,[[63]](#cite_note-65) and there is no general agreement as to whether the bull was genuine or a forgery.[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67) Further, it had no standing in the Irish legal system.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Ireland_1450.png/250px-Ireland_1450.png)](/wiki/File:Ireland_1450.png)

Political boundaries in Ireland in 1450, before the [plantations](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland")

In 1172, Pope [Alexander III](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_III "Pope Alexander III") further encouraged Henry to advance the integration of the Irish Church with Rome. Henry was authorised to impose a tithe of one penny per hearth as an annual contribution. This church levy, called [Peter's Pence](/wiki/Peter%27s_Pence "Peter's Pence"), is extant in Ireland as a voluntary donation. In turn, Henry assumed the title of [Lord of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_of_Ireland "Lord of Ireland") which Henry conferred on his younger son, [John Lackland](/wiki/John_Lackland "John Lackland"), in 1185. This defined the Anglo-Norman administration in Ireland as the [Lordship of Ireland](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ireland "Lordship of Ireland").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When Henry's successor, [Richard the Lionheart](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") died unexpectedly in 1199, [John](/wiki/John_of_England "John of England") inherited the crown of England and retained the Lordship of Ireland. Over the century that followed, Norman feudal law gradually replaced the Gaelic Brehon Law across large areas, so that by the late 13th century the [Norman-Irish](/wiki/Hiberno-Norman "Hiberno-Norman") had established a feudal system throughout much of Ireland. Norman settlements were characterised by the establishment of baronies, manors, towns and the seeds of the modern county system. A version of [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") (the [Great Charter of Ireland](/wiki/Great_Charter_of_Ireland "Great Charter of Ireland")), substituting *Dublin* for *London* and the *Irish Church* for, the English church at the time, the *Catholic Church*, was published in 1216 and the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") was founded in 1297.

### Gaelicisation

From the mid-14th century, after the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), Norman settlements in Ireland went into a period of decline. The Norman rulers and the Gaelic Irish elites intermarried and the areas under Norman rule became [Gaelicised](/wiki/Gaelicisation "Gaelicisation"). In some parts, a hybrid Hiberno-Norman culture emerged. In response, the [Irish parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland#Middle_Ages "Parliament of Ireland") passed the [Statutes of Kilkenny](/wiki/Statutes_of_Kilkenny "Statutes of Kilkenny") in 1367. These were a set of laws designed to prevent the assimilation of the Normans into Irish society by requiring English subjects in Ireland to speak English, follow English customs and abide by English law.[[66]](#cite_note-68)

By the end of the 15th century, central English authority in Ireland had all but disappeared, and a renewed Irish culture and language, albeit with Norman influences, was again dominant. English Crown control remained relatively unshaken in an amorphous foothold around Dublin known as [The Pale](/wiki/The_Pale "The Pale"), and under the provisions of [Poynings' Law](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(on_certification_of_acts) "Poynings' Law (on certification of acts)") of 1494, Irish Parliamentary legislation was subject to the approval of the [English Privy Council](/wiki/English_Privy_Council "English Privy Council").[[67]](#cite_note-69)

### Kingdom of Ireland

Main article: [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-25_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-25_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-25_(cropped).jpg)

A 16th-century perception of Irish women and girls, illustrated in the manuscript "Théâtre de tous les peuples et nations de la terre avec leurs habits et ornemens divers, tant anciens que modernes, diligemment depeints au naturel". Painted by [Lucas d'Heere](/wiki/Lucas_de_Heere "Lucas de Heere") in the 2nd half of the 16th century. Preserved in the [Ghent University Library](/wiki/Ghent_University_Library "Ghent University Library").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

The title of [King of Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") was re-created in 1542 by [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII"), [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England"), of the [Tudor dynasty](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty"). English rule was reinforced and expanded in Ireland during the latter part of the 16th century, leading to the [Tudor conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Tudor_conquest_of_Ireland "Tudor conquest of Ireland"). A near-complete conquest was achieved by the turn of the 17th century, following the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War_(Ireland) "Nine Years' War (Ireland)") and the [Flight of the Earls](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Earls "Flight of the Earls").

This control was consolidated during the wars and conflicts of the 17th century, including the English and Scottish colonisation in the [Plantations of Ireland](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland"), the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms") and the [Williamite War](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland"). Irish losses during the Wars of the Three Kingdoms (which, in Ireland, included the [Irish Confederacy](/wiki/Irish_Confederacy "Irish Confederacy") and the [Cromwellian conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Cromwellian_conquest_of_Ireland "Cromwellian conquest of Ireland")) are estimated to include 20,000 battlefield casualties. 200,000 civilians are estimated to have died as a result of a combination of war-related famine, displacement, guerrilla activity and pestilence throughout the war. A further 50,000[[c]](#cite_note-73) were sent into [indentured servitude](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude") in the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"). Physician-general [William Petty](/wiki/William_Petty "William Petty") estimated that 504,000 Catholic Irish and 112,000 Protestant settlers died, and 100,000 people were transported, as a result of the war.[[71]](#cite_note-74) If a prewar population of 1.5 million is assumed, this would mean that the population was reduced by almost half.

The religious struggles of the 17th century left a deep sectarian division in Ireland. Religious allegiance now determined the perception in law of loyalty to the Irish King and Parliament. After the passing of [the Test Act 1672](/wiki/Test_Act#Test_Act_of_1673 "Test Act"), and the victory of the forces of the dual monarchy of [William](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") over the [Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism"), Roman Catholics and nonconforming Protestant Dissenters were barred from sitting as members in the Irish Parliament. Under the emerging [Penal Laws](/wiki/Penal_Laws_(Ireland) "Penal Laws (Ireland)"), Irish Roman Catholics and Dissenters were increasingly deprived of various civil rights, even the ownership of hereditary property. Additional regressive punitive legislation followed in 1703, 1709 and 1728. This completed a comprehensive systemic effort to materially disadvantage Roman Catholics and Protestant Dissenters while enriching a new ruling class of Anglican conformists.[[72]](#cite_note-75) The new Anglo-Irish ruling class became known as the [Protestant Ascendancy](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b5/Half-hanging_on_travelling_gallows.gif)](/wiki/File:Half-hanging_on_travelling_gallows.gif)

[Half-hanging](/wiki/Half-hanging "Half-hanging") of suspected [United Irishmen](/wiki/United_Irishmen "United Irishmen")

The "[Great Frost](/wiki/Irish_Famine_(1740%E2%80%931741)#Cause "Irish Famine (1740–1741)")" struck Ireland and the rest of Europe between December 1739 and September 1741, after a decade of relatively mild winters. The winters destroyed stored crops of potatoes and other staples, and the poor summers severely damaged harvests.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] This resulted in the [famine of 1740](/wiki/Irish_Famine_(1740%E2%80%931741) "Irish Famine (1740–1741)"). An estimated 250,000 people (about one in eight of the population) died from the ensuing pestilence and disease.[[74]](#cite_note-cormac_famine-77) The Irish government halted export of corn and kept the army in quarters but did little more.[[74]](#cite_note-cormac_famine-77)[[75]](#cite_note-crawford_feast-78) Local gentry and charitable organisations provided relief but could do little to prevent the ensuing mortality.[[74]](#cite_note-cormac_famine-77)[[75]](#cite_note-crawford_feast-78)

In the aftermath of the famine, an increase in industrial production and a surge in trade brought a succession of construction booms. The population soared in the latter part of this century and the [architectural legacy of Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") Ireland was built. In 1782, [Poynings' Law](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(on_certification_of_acts) "Poynings' Law (on certification of acts)") was repealed, giving Ireland legislative independence from Great Britain for the first time since 1495. The British government, however, still retained the right to nominate the government of Ireland without the consent of the Irish parliament.

### 1798 Rebellion

Main article: [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/MAXWELL%281845%29_p130_Camp_on_Vinegar_Hill.jpg/250px-MAXWELL%281845%29_p130_Camp_on_Vinegar_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:MAXWELL(1845)_p130_Camp_on_Vinegar_Hill.jpg)

"The Camp on Vinegar Hill" - an illustration by [George Cruikshank](/wiki/George_Cruikshank "George Cruikshank") to accompany [William Hamilton Maxwell](/wiki/William_Hamilton_Maxwell "William Hamilton Maxwell")'s 1845 work *History of the Irish rebellion in 1798*

In 1798, members of the Protestant Dissenter tradition (mainly [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism")) made common cause with Roman Catholics in a republican rebellion inspired and led by the [Society of United Irishmen](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen"), with the aim of creating an independent Ireland. Despite assistance from France the [rebellion](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798") was put down by British and Irish government and yeomanry forces. The rebellion lasted from the 24th of May to the 12th of October that year and saw the establishment of the short lived [Irish Republic (1798)](/wiki/Irish_Republic_(1798) "Irish Republic (1798)") in the province of [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht "Connacht"). It saw numerous battles across the island with an estimated 30,000 people dead.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Union with Great Britain

Main article: [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")

As a direct result of the 1798 rebellion in its aftermath in 1800, the British and Irish parliaments both passed [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") that, with effect from 1 January 1801, merged the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") and the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") to create a [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland").[[76]](#cite_note-Ward_1994_28-79)

The passage of the Act in the Irish Parliament was ultimately achieved with substantial majorities, having failed on the first attempt in 1799. According to contemporary documents and historical analysis, this was achieved through a considerable degree of bribery, with funding provided by the British Secret Service Office, and the awarding of peerages, places and honours to secure votes.[[76]](#cite_note-Ward_1994_28-79) Thus, the parliament in Ireland was abolished and replaced by a [united parliament at Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in London, though resistance remained, as evidenced by [Robert Emmet](/wiki/Robert_Emmet "Robert Emmet")'s failed [Irish Rebellion of 1803](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1803 "Irish Rebellion of 1803").

Aside from the development of the [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") industry, Ireland was largely passed over by the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), partly because it lacked coal and iron resources[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81) and partly because of the impact of the sudden union with the structurally superior economy of England,[[79]](#cite_note-82) which saw Ireland as a source of agricultural produce and capital.[[80]](#cite_note-83)[[81]](#cite_note-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/FRENCH%281891%29_p118_THE_FAMINE_AT_BOFIN.jpg/250px-FRENCH%281891%29_p118_THE_FAMINE_AT_BOFIN.jpg)](/wiki/File:FRENCH(1891)_p118_THE_FAMINE_AT_BOFIN.jpg)

A depiction of the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") from *Our Boys in Ireland* by Henry Willard French (1891)

The [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") of 1845–1851 devastated Ireland, as in those years Ireland's population fell by one-third. More than one million people died from starvation and disease, with an additional million people emigrating during the famine, mostly to the United States and Canada.[[82]](#cite_note-85) In the century that followed, an economic depression caused by the famine resulted in a further million people emigrating.[[83]](#cite_note-86) By the end of the decade, half of all [immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") was from Ireland. The period of civil unrest that followed until the end of the 19th century is referred to as the [Land War](/wiki/Land_War "Land War"). Mass emigration became deeply entrenched and the population continued to decline until the mid-20th century. Immediately prior to the famine the population was recorded as 8.2 million by the [1841 census](/wiki/1841_census_of_Ireland "1841 census of Ireland").[[84]](#cite_note-87) The population has never returned to this level since.[[85]](#cite_note-88) The population continued to fall until 1961; [County Leitrim](/wiki/County_Leitrim "County Leitrim") was the final Irish county to record a population increase post-famine, in 2006.

The 19th and early 20th centuries saw the rise of modern [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"), primarily among the Roman Catholic population. The pre-eminent Irish political figure after the Union was [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell"). He was elected as Member of Parliament for [Ennis](/wiki/Ennis_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Ennis (UK Parliament constituency)") in a surprise result and despite being unable to take his seat [as a Roman Catholic](/wiki/The_Test_Act "The Test Act"). O'Connell spearheaded a vigorous campaign that was taken up by the Prime Minister, the Irish-born soldier and statesman, the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington"). Steering the [Catholic Relief Bill](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829") through Parliament, aided by future prime minister [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"), Wellington prevailed upon a reluctant [George IV](/wiki/George_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "George IV of the United Kingdom") to sign the Bill and proclaim it into law. [George's father](/wiki/George_III "George III") had opposed the plan of the earlier Prime Minister, [Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"), to introduce such a bill following the Union of 1801, fearing [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") to be in conflict with the [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701").

Daniel O'Connell led a subsequent campaign, for the repeal of the Act of Union, which failed. Later in the century, [Charles Stewart Parnell](/wiki/Charles_Stewart_Parnell "Charles Stewart Parnell") and others campaigned for autonomy within the Union, or "[Home Rule](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_Bills "Irish Home Rule Bills")". Unionists, especially those located in Ulster, were strongly opposed to Home Rule, which they thought would be dominated by Catholic interests.[[86]](#cite_note-89) After several attempts to pass a Home Rule bill through parliament, it looked certain that one would finally pass in 1914. To prevent this from happening, the [Ulster Volunteers](/wiki/Ulster_Volunteers "Ulster Volunteers") were formed in 1913 under the leadership of [Edward Carson](/wiki/Edward_Carson "Edward Carson").[[87]](#cite_note-Kee_1972_478–530-90)

Their formation was followed in 1914 by the establishment of the [Irish Volunteers](/wiki/Irish_Volunteers "Irish Volunteers"), whose aim was to ensure that the [Home Rule Bill](/wiki/Third_Home_Rule_Act "Third Home Rule Act") was passed. The Act was passed but with the "temporary" exclusion of the six counties of Ulster, which later became Northern Ireland. Before it could be implemented, however, the Act was suspended for the duration of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). The Irish Volunteers split into two groups. The majority, approximately 175,000 in number, under [John Redmond](/wiki/John_Redmond "John Redmond"), took the name [National Volunteers](/wiki/National_Volunteers "National Volunteers") and supported [Irish involvement](/wiki/Ireland_and_World_War_I "Ireland and World War I") in the war. A minority, approximately 13,000, retained the Irish Volunteers' name and opposed Ireland's involvement in the war.[[87]](#cite_note-Kee_1972_478–530-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Sackville_Street_%28Dublin%29_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG/250px-Sackville_Street_%28Dublin%29_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sackville_Street_(Dublin)_after_the_1916_Easter_Rising.JPG)

Sackville Street (now [O'Connell Street](/wiki/O%27Connell_Street "O'Connell Street")), Dublin, after the 1916 [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising")

The [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") of 1916 was carried out by the latter group together with a smaller socialist militia, the [Irish Citizen Army](/wiki/Irish_Citizen_Army "Irish Citizen Army"). The British response, executing 15 leaders of the Rising over a period of ten days and imprisoning or interning more than a thousand people, turned the mood of the country in favour of the rebels. Support for [Irish republicanism increased](/wiki/Physical_force_Irish_republicanism "Physical force Irish republicanism") further due to the ongoing war in Europe, as well as the [Conscription Crisis of 1918](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1918 "Conscription Crisis of 1918").[[88]](#cite_note-autogenerated34-91)

The pro-independence republican party, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), received overwhelming endorsement in the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland"), and in 1919 proclaimed an [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic"), setting up its own parliament (*[Dáil Éireann](/wiki/First_D%C3%A1il "First Dáil")*) and government. Simultaneously the Volunteers, which became known as the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army "Irish Republican Army") (IRA), launched a [three-year guerrilla war](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"), which ended in a truce in July 1921 (although violence continued until June 1922, mostly in Northern Ireland).[[88]](#cite_note-autogenerated34-91)

### Partition

Main article: [Partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland")

In December 1921, the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") was concluded between the British government and representatives of the [Second Dáil](/wiki/Second_D%C3%A1il "Second Dáil"). It gave Ireland complete independence in its home affairs and practical independence for foreign policy, but an opt-out clause allowed [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") to remain within the United Kingdom, which it immediately exercised. Additionally, Members of the [Free State Parliament](/wiki/Third_D%C3%A1il "Third Dáil") were required to swear [an oath of allegiance to the Constitution of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Ireland) "Oath of Allegiance (Ireland)") and make a statement of faithfulness to the king.[[89]](#cite_note-92) Disagreements over these provisions led to a split in the nationalist movement and a subsequent [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War") between the new government of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") and those opposed to the treaty, led by [Éamon de Valera](/wiki/%C3%89amon_de_Valera "Éamon de Valera"). The civil war officially ended in May 1923 when de Valera issued a cease-fire order.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

#### Independence

Main articles: [History of the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/History_of_the_Republic_of_Ireland "History of the Republic of Ireland") and [Economy of the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Economy of the Republic of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Anglo-Irish_Treaty_Griffith_annotated2.gif/250px-Anglo-Irish_Treaty_Griffith_annotated2.gif)](/wiki/File:Anglo-Irish_Treaty_Griffith_annotated2.gif)

Annotated page from the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") that established the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") and independence for 26 out of 32 [Irish counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland")

During its first decade, the newly formed Irish Free State was governed by the victors of the civil war. When de Valera achieved power, he took advantage of the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") and [political circumstances](/wiki/Edward_VIII_abdication_crisis "Edward VIII abdication crisis") to build upon inroads to greater sovereignty made by the previous government. The oath was abolished and in 1937 a new constitution was adopted.[[88]](#cite_note-autogenerated34-91) This completed a process of gradual separation from the British Empire that governments had pursued since independence. However, it was not until 1949 that the state was declared, officially, to be the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").

[The state was neutral](/wiki/Irish_neutrality "Irish neutrality") during [World War II](/wiki/The_Emergency_(Ireland) "The Emergency (Ireland)"), but offered [clandestine assistance to the Allies](/wiki/Irish_neutrality_during_World_War_II "Irish neutrality during World War II"), particularly in the potential defence of Northern Ireland. Despite their country's neutrality, approximately 50,000[[91]](#cite_note-94) volunteers from independent Ireland joined the British forces during the war, four being awarded [Victoria Crosses](/wiki/Victoria_Crosses "Victoria Crosses").

The [German intelligence](/wiki/Abwehr "Abwehr") was also active in Ireland.[[92]](#cite_note-autogenerated695-95) Its operations ended in September 1941 when [police](/wiki/Garda_S%C3%ADoch%C3%A1na "Garda Síochána") made arrests based on surveillance carried out on the key diplomatic legations in Dublin. To the authorities, counterintelligence was a fundamental line of defence. With a regular army of only slightly over seven thousand men at the start of the war, and with limited supplies of modern weapons, the state would have had great difficulty in defending itself from invasion from either side in the conflict.[[92]](#cite_note-autogenerated695-95)[[93]](#cite_note-96)

Large-scale emigration marked most of the post-WWII period (particularly during the 1950s and 1980s), but beginning in 1987 the economy improved, and the 1990s saw the beginning of substantial economic growth. This period of growth became known as the [Celtic Tiger](/wiki/Celtic_Tiger "Celtic Tiger").[[94]](#cite_note-clancy3-97) The Republic's real GDP grew by an average of 9.6% per annum between 1995 and 1999,[[95]](#cite_note-98) in which year the Republic joined the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"). In 2000, it was the sixth-richest country in the world in terms of GDP per capita.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Historian [R. F. Foster](/wiki/R._F._Foster_(historian) "R. F. Foster (historian)") argues the cause was a combination of a new sense of initiative and the entry of American corporations. He concludes the chief factors were low taxation, pro-business regulatory policies, and a young, tech-savvy workforce. For many multinationals, the decision to do business in Ireland was made easier still by generous incentives from the [Industrial Development Authority](/wiki/IDA_Ireland "IDA Ireland"). In addition [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") membership was helpful, giving the country lucrative access to markets that it had previously reached only through the United Kingdom, and pumping huge subsidies and investment capital into the Irish economy.[[97]](#cite_note-100)

Modernisation brought secularisation in its wake. The traditionally high levels of religiosity have sharply declined. Foster points to three factors: First, Irish feminism, largely imported from America with liberal stances on contraception, abortion and divorce, undermined the authority of bishops and priests. Second, the mishandling of the paedophile scandals humiliated the Church, whose bishops seemed less concerned with the victims and more concerned with covering up for errant priests. Third, prosperity brought hedonism and materialism that undercut the ideals of saintly poverty.[[98]](#cite_note-101)

The [post-2008 Irish economic downturn](/wiki/Post-2008_Irish_economic_downturn "Post-2008 Irish economic downturn") dramatically ended this period of boom. GDP fell by 3% in 2008 and by 7.1% in 2009, the worst year since records began (although earnings by foreign-owned businesses continued to grow).[[99]](#cite_note-102) The state has since experienced deep recession, with unemployment, which doubled during 2009, remaining above 14% in 2012.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

#### Northern Ireland

Main articles: [History of Northern Ireland](/wiki/History_of_Northern_Ireland "History of Northern Ireland") and [Economy of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_Northern_Ireland "Economy of Northern Ireland")

Northern Ireland resulted from the division of the United Kingdom by the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920"), and until 1972 was a self-governing jurisdiction within the United Kingdom with its own parliament and prime minister. Northern Ireland, as part of the United Kingdom, was not neutral during the Second World War, and [Belfast suffered four bombing raids](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz") in 1941. [Conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") was not extended to Northern Ireland, and roughly an equal number volunteered from Northern Ireland as volunteered from the Republic of Ireland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/de/Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg/250px-Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carson_signing_Solemn_League_and_Covenant.jpg)

[Edward Carson](/wiki/Edward_Carson "Edward Carson") signing the [Solemn League and Covenant](/wiki/Ulster_Covenant "Ulster Covenant") in 1912, declaring opposition to [Home Rule](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_bills "Irish Home Rule bills") "using all means which may be found necessary"

Although Northern Ireland was largely spared the strife of the civil war, in the decades that followed partition there were sporadic episodes of inter-communal violence. Nationalists, mainly Roman Catholic, wanted to unite Ireland as an independent republic, whereas unionists, mainly Protestant, wanted Northern Ireland to remain in the United Kingdom. The Protestant and Catholic communities in Northern Ireland voted largely along [sectarian](/wiki/Sectarian "Sectarian") lines, meaning that the government of Northern Ireland (elected by ["first-past-the-post"](/wiki/Plurality_electoral_system "Plurality electoral system") from 1929) was controlled by the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party"). Over time, the minority Catholic community felt increasingly alienated with further disaffection fuelled by practices such as [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering") and discrimination in housing and employment.[[101]](#cite_note-whyte-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105)[[103]](#cite_note-106)

In the late 1960s, nationalist grievances were aired publicly in mass civil rights protests, which were often confronted by [loyalist](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") counter-protests.[[104]](#cite_note-107) The government's reaction to confrontations was seen to be one-sided and heavy-handed in favour of unionists. Law and order broke down as unrest and inter-communal violence increased.[[105]](#cite_note-108) The Northern Ireland government requested the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") to aid the police and protect the [Irish Nationalist](/wiki/Irish_Nationalist "Irish Nationalist") population. In 1969, the paramilitary [Provisional IRA](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army"), which favoured the creation of a [united Ireland](/wiki/United_Ireland "United Ireland"), emerged from a split in the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1922%E2%80%931969) "Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)") and began a campaign against what it called the "British occupation of the six counties".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Other groups, both the unionist and nationalist participated in violence, and a period known as "[the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles")" began. More than 3,600 deaths resulted over the subsequent three decades of conflict.[[106]](#cite_note-109) Owing to the civil unrest during the Troubles, the British government suspended home rule in 1972 and imposed [direct rule](/wiki/Direct_rule_over_Northern_Ireland "Direct rule over Northern Ireland"). There were several unsuccessful attempts to end the Troubles politically, such as the [Sunningdale Agreement](/wiki/Sunningdale_Agreement "Sunningdale Agreement") of 1973. In 1998, following a ceasefire by the Provisional IRA and multi-party talks, the [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement") was concluded as a treaty between the British and Irish governments, annexing the text agreed in the multi-party talks.

The substance of the Agreement (formally referred to as the Belfast Agreement) was later endorsed by referendums in both parts of Ireland. The Agreement restored self-government to Northern Ireland on the basis of power-sharing in a regional [Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") drawn from the major parties in a new [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), with entrenched protections for the two main communities. The Executive is jointly headed by a [First Minister and deputy First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister "First Minister and deputy First Minister") drawn from the unionist and nationalist parties. Violence had decreased greatly after the Provisional IRA and loyalist ceasefires in 1994, and in 2005, the Provisional IRA announced the end of its armed campaign and an [independent commission](/wiki/Independent_International_Commission_on_Decommissioning "Independent International Commission on Decommissioning") supervised its disarmament and that of other nationalist and unionist paramilitary organisations.[[107]](#cite_note-110)

The Assembly and power-sharing Executive were suspended several times but were restored again in 2007. In that year the British government officially ended its military support of the police in Northern Ireland ([Operation Banner](/wiki/Operation_Banner "Operation Banner")) and began withdrawing troops. On 27 June 2012, Northern Ireland's deputy first minister and former IRA commander, [Martin McGuinness](/wiki/Martin_McGuinness "Martin McGuinness"), shook hands with Queen Elizabeth II in Belfast, symbolising reconciliation between the two sides.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

## Politics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/British%E2%80%93European_Union_frontier_in_Ireland.svg/250px-British%E2%80%93European_Union_frontier_in_Ireland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British%E2%80%93European_Union_frontier_in_Ireland.svg)

Political entities on the island of Ireland

The island is divided between the Republic of Ireland, an independent [state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), and Northern Ireland, a constituent country of the United Kingdom. They share an [open border](/wiki/Irish_border "Irish border") and both are part of the [Common Travel Area](/wiki/Common_Travel_Area "Common Travel Area") and as a consequence, there is [free movement of people, goods, services and capital](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") across the border.

The Republic of Ireland is a member state of the European Union while the United Kingdom is a former member state, having both acceded to its precursor entity, the European Economic Community (EEC), in 1973 but the UK [left the European Union in 2020](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") after a [referendum on EU membership was held in 2016](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") which resulted in 51.9% of UK voters choosing to leave the bloc.

### Republic of Ireland

Main article: [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/%C3%81ras_an_Uachtar%C3%A1in-2011.jpg/250px-%C3%81ras_an_Uachtar%C3%A1in-2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%81ras_an_Uachtar%C3%A1in-2011.jpg)

*[Áras an Uachtaráin](/wiki/%C3%81ras_an_Uachtar%C3%A1in "Áras an Uachtaráin")*, the official residence of the [President of Ireland](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland")

The Republic of Ireland is a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") based on the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), with a [written constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") and a popularly elected [president](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland") whose role is mostly ceremonial. The [Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas "Oireachtas") is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") parliament, composed of [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann "Dáil Éireann") (the Dáil), a house of representatives, and [Seanad Éireann](/wiki/Seanad_%C3%89ireann "Seanad Éireann") (the Seanad), an [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"). The [government](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland") is headed by a prime minister, the [Taoiseach](/wiki/Taoiseach "Taoiseach"), who is appointed by the president on the nomination of the Dáil. Its capital is Dublin.

The Republic of Ireland today ranks among the wealthiest countries in the world in terms of [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita")[[109]](#cite_note-112) and in 2015 was ranked the sixth most developed nation in the world by the United Nations' [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index").[[110]](#cite_note-113) A period of rapid economic expansion from 1995 onwards became known as the [Celtic Tiger](/wiki/Celtic_Tiger "Celtic Tiger") period, was brought to an end by the post-2008 Irish economic downturn and an economic depression in 2009. According to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), Ireland is the second most peaceful country in the world.[[111]](#cite_note-114)

### Northern Ireland

Main article: [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/StormontCarson.jpg/250px-StormontCarson.jpg)](/wiki/File:StormontCarson.jpg)

[Parliament Buildings](/wiki/Parliament_Buildings_(Northern_Ireland) "Parliament Buildings (Northern Ireland)"), in [Stormont Estate](/wiki/Stormont_Estate "Stormont Estate"), seat of the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly")

Northern Ireland is a part of the United Kingdom with a local [executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") and [assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") which exercise devolved powers. The executive is jointly headed by the first and deputy first minister, with the ministries being allocated in proportion to each party's representation in the assembly. Its capital is [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast").

Ultimately political power is held by the [UK government](/wiki/UK_government "UK government"), from which Northern Ireland has gone through intermittent periods of direct rule during which devolved powers have been suspended. Northern Ireland elects 18 of the UK [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom")' 650 MPs. The [Northern Ireland Secretary](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Secretary "Northern Ireland Secretary") is a cabinet-level post in the British government.

Along with [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") and with Scotland, Northern Ireland forms one of the three separate legal jurisdictions of the UK, all of which share the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") as their court of final appeal.

### All-island institutions

As part of the Good Friday Agreement, the British and Irish governments agreed on the creation of all-island institutions and areas of cooperation. The [North/South Ministerial Council](/wiki/North/South_Ministerial_Council "North/South Ministerial Council") is an institution through which ministers from the Government of Ireland and the Northern Ireland Executive agree all-island policies. At least six of these policy areas must have an associated all-island "implementation body", and at least six others must be implemented separately in each jurisdiction. The implementation bodies are: [Waterways Ireland](/wiki/Waterways_Ireland "Waterways Ireland"), the [Food Safety Promotion Board](/wiki/Food_Safety_Promotion_Board "Food Safety Promotion Board"), [InterTradeIreland](/wiki/InterTradeIreland "InterTradeIreland"), the [Special European Union Programmes Body](/wiki/Special_European_Union_Programmes_Body "Special European Union Programmes Body"), [the North/South Language Body](/wiki/The_North/South_Language_Body "The North/South Language Body") and the [Foyle, Carlingford and Irish Lights Commission](/wiki/Commissioners_of_Irish_Lights "Commissioners of Irish Lights").

The [British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Intergovernmental_Conference "British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference") provides for co-operation between the Government of Ireland and the Government of the United Kingdom on all matters of mutual interest, especially Northern Ireland. In light of the Republic's particular interest in the governance of Northern Ireland, "regular and frequent" meetings co-chaired by the Irish Minister for Foreign Affairs and the British Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, dealing with non-devolved matters to do with Northern Ireland and non-devolved [all-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") issues, are required to take place under the establishing treaty.

The [North/South Inter-Parliamentary Association](/wiki/North/South_Inter-Parliamentary_Association "North/South Inter-Parliamentary Association") is a joint parliamentary forum for the island of Ireland. It has no formal powers but operates as a forum for discussing matters of common concern between the respective legislatures.

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Ireland](/wiki/Geography_of_Ireland "Geography of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Ireland_physical_large.png/330px-Ireland_physical_large.png)](/wiki/File:Ireland_physical_large.png)

Physical features of Ireland

Ireland is located in the [north-west of Europe](/wiki/North-West_Europe "North-West Europe"), between latitudes [51°](/wiki/51st_parallel_north "51st parallel north") and [56° N](/wiki/56th_parallel_north "56th parallel north"), and longitudes [11°](/wiki/11th_meridian_west "11th meridian west") and [5° W](/wiki/5th_meridian_west "5th meridian west"). It is separated from Great Britain by the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") and the [North Channel](/wiki/North_Channel_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "North Channel (Great Britain and Ireland)"), which has a width of 23 kilometres (14 mi)[[112]](#cite_note-115) at its narrowest point. To the west is the northern Atlantic Ocean and to the south is the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea"), which lies between Ireland and [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), in France. Ireland has a total area of 84,421 km2 (32,595 mi2),[[1]](#cite_note-irlgeog-1)[[2]](#cite_note-royle-2)[[113]](#cite_note-116) of which the Republic of Ireland occupies 83 percent.[[114]](#cite_note-117) Ireland and Great Britain, together with many nearby smaller islands, are known collectively as the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles").[[115]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-118) As [the term *British Isles* can be controversial](/wiki/British_Isles_naming_dispute "British Isles naming dispute") in relation to Ireland, the alternate term *Britain and Ireland* is sometimes used as a neutral term for the islands.[[116]](#cite_note-GuardianMOS01-119)

A ring of coastal mountains surrounds low plains at the centre of the island. The highest of these is [Carrauntoohil](/wiki/Carrauntoohil "Carrauntoohil") ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Corrán Tuathail*) in [County Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry"), which rises to 1,039 m (3,409 ft) above sea level.[[117]](#cite_note-OSI_faqs-120) The most arable land lies in the province of [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster "Leinster").[[118]](#cite_note-121) Western areas are mainly mountainous and rocky with green panoramic vistas. [River Shannon](/wiki/River_Shannon "River Shannon"), the island's longest river at 360.5 km (224 mi) long, rises in [County Cavan](/wiki/County_Cavan "County Cavan") in the north-west and flows through [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") in the midwest.[[117]](#cite_note-OSI_faqs-120)[[119]](#cite_note-122)

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Ireland](/wiki/Geology_of_Ireland "Geology of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Topography_Ireland.jpg/250px-Topography_Ireland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Topography_Ireland.jpg)

[Topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") of Ireland

The island consists of varied [geological provinces](/wiki/Geologic_province "Geologic province"). In the west, around County Galway and [County Donegal](/wiki/County_Donegal "County Donegal"), is a medium- to high-grade metamorphic and igneous complex of [Caledonide](/wiki/Caledonides "Caledonides") affinity, similar to the [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"). Across southeast Ulster and extending southwest to [Longford](/wiki/Longford "Longford") and south to [Navan](/wiki/Navan "Navan") is a province of [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician") and [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") rocks, with similarities to the [Southern Uplands](/wiki/Southern_Uplands "Southern Uplands") province of Scotland. Further south, along the [County Wexford](/wiki/County_Wexford "County Wexford") coastline, is an area of granite [intrusives](/wiki/Intrusion "Intrusion") into more Ordovician and Silurian rocks, like that found in Wales.[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-124)

In the southwest, around [Bantry Bay](/wiki/Bantry_Bay "Bantry Bay") and the mountains of [MacGillycuddy's Reeks](/wiki/MacGillycuddy%27s_Reeks "MacGillycuddy's Reeks"), is an area of substantially deformed, lightly [metamorphosed](/wiki/Metamorphic_rock "Metamorphic rock") [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian")-aged rocks.[[122]](#cite_note-125) This partial ring of "hard rock" geology is covered by a blanket of [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") limestone over the centre of the country, giving rise to a comparatively fertile and lush landscape. The west-coast district of [the Burren](/wiki/The_Burren "The Burren") around [Lisdoonvarna](/wiki/Lisdoonvarna "Lisdoonvarna") has well-developed [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") features.[[123]](#cite_note-126) Significant stratiform lead-zinc mineralisation is found in the limestones around [Silvermines](/wiki/Silvermines "Silvermines") and [Tynagh](/wiki/Tynagh "Tynagh").

[Hydrocarbon exploration](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_exploration "Hydrocarbon exploration") is ongoing following the first major find at the [Kinsale Head gas field](/wiki/Kinsale_Head_gas_field "Kinsale Head gas field") off [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)") in the mid-1970s.[[124]](#cite_note-energyfiles-127)[[125]](#cite_note-sch-128) In 1999, economically significant finds of natural gas were made in the [Corrib Gas Field](/wiki/Corrib_Gas_Field "Corrib Gas Field") off the County Mayo coast. This has increased activity off the west coast in parallel with the "[West of Shetland](/wiki/North_Sea_oil#West_of_Shetland "North Sea oil")" step-out development from the [North Sea hydrocarbon province](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil"). In 2000, the [Helvick](/wiki/Helvick "Helvick") oil field was discovered, which was estimated to contain over 28 million barrels (4,500,000 m3) of oil.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Ireland](/wiki/Climate_of_Ireland "Climate of Ireland")

The island's lush vegetation, a product of its mild climate and frequent rainfall, earns it the sobriquet *the Emerald Isle*. Overall, Ireland has a mild but changeable [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") with few extremes. The climate is typically insular and [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), avoiding the extremes in temperature of many other areas in the world at similar latitudes.[[127]](#cite_note-MEclimate-130) This is a result of the moist winds which ordinarily prevail from the southwestern Atlantic.

Precipitation falls throughout the year but is light overall, particularly in the east. The west tends to be wetter on average and prone to Atlantic storms, especially in the late autumn and winter months. These occasionally bring destructive winds and higher total rainfall to these areas, as well as sometimes snow and hail. The regions of north County Galway and east County Mayo have the highest incidents of recorded lightning annually for the island, with lightning occurring approximately five to ten days per year in these areas.[[128]](#cite_note-MErainfall-131) [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"), in the south, records the least snow whereas [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), in the north, records the most.

Inland areas are warmer in summer and colder in winter. Usually around 40 days of the year are below freezing 0 °C (32 °F) at inland [weather stations](/wiki/Weather_station "Weather station"), compared to 10 days at coastal stations. Ireland is sometimes affected by heat waves, most recently in 1995, [2003](/wiki/2003_European_heat_wave "2003 European heat wave"), [2006](/wiki/2006_European_heat_wave#Ireland "2006 European heat wave"), 2013 and [2018](/wiki/2018_British_Isles_heat_wave "2018 British Isles heat wave"). In common with the rest of Europe, Ireland experienced unusually cold weather during [the winter of 2010–11](/wiki/Winter_of_2010%E2%80%9311_in_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Winter of 2010–11 in Great Britain and Ireland"). Temperatures fell as low as −17.2 °C (1 °F) in County Mayo on 20 December[[129]](#cite_note-132) and up to a metre (3 ft) of snow fell in mountainous areas.
