## Flora and fauna

Main articles: [Fauna of Ireland](/wiki/Fauna_of_Ireland "Fauna of Ireland"), [Flora of Ireland](/wiki/Flora_of_Ireland "Flora of Ireland"), and [Trees of Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Trees_of_Britain_and_Ireland "Trees of Britain and Ireland")

[![The red fox is common in Ireland.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Red_Fox_%28Vulpes_vulpes%29_%284%29.jpg/250px-Red_Fox_%28Vulpes_vulpes%29_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Fox_(Vulpes_vulpes)_(4).jpg)

Two [red foxes](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox") (*Vulpes vulpes*) in Gubbeen, County Cork

Unlike Great Britain which had a land bridge with [mainland Europe](/wiki/Mainland_Europe "Mainland Europe"), Ireland only had an ice bridge ending around 14,000 years ago at the end of the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") and as a result, it has fewer land animal and plant species than Great Britain or mainland Europe.[[20]](#cite_note-Marine_Geology-22)[[21]](#cite_note-drowning-23) There are [55 mammal species](/wiki/List_of_mammals_of_Ireland "List of mammals of Ireland") in Ireland, and of them, only 26 land mammal species are considered native to Ireland.[[132]](#cite_note-Costello,_M.J_93-135) Some species, such as, the [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [hedgehog](/wiki/European_hedgehog "European hedgehog") and [badger](/wiki/European_badger "European badger"), are very common, whereas others, like the [Irish hare](/wiki/Mountain_hare "Mountain hare"), [red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer") and [pine marten](/wiki/Pine_marten "Pine marten") are less so. Aquatic wildlife, such as species of sea turtle, shark, seal, whale, and dolphin, are common off the coast. About 400 species of birds have been recorded in Ireland. Many of these are migratory, including the [barn swallow](/wiki/Barn_swallow "Barn swallow").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Irl-female_red_deer_Killarney.jpg/250px-Irl-female_red_deer_Killarney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irl-female_red_deer_Killarney.jpg)

[Red deer](/wiki/Red_deer "Red deer") (*Cervus elaphus*) in [Killarney National Park](/wiki/Killarney_National_Park "Killarney National Park")

Several different habitat types are found in Ireland, including farmland, open woodland, [temperate broadleaf and mixed forests](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forests "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forests"), [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") plantations, [peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") bogs and a variety of coastal habitats. However, agriculture drives current land use patterns in Ireland, limiting natural habitat preserves,[[133]](#cite_note-www2000-136) particularly for larger wild mammals with greater territorial needs. With no large [apex predators](/wiki/Apex_predator "Apex predator") in Ireland other than humans and dogs, such populations of animals as semi-wild deer that cannot be controlled by smaller predators, such as the fox, are controlled by annual [culling](/wiki/Culling "Culling").

There are no snakes in Ireland, and only one species of reptile (the [common lizard](/wiki/Viviparous_lizard "Viviparous lizard")) is native to the island. Extinct species include the [Irish elk](/wiki/Irish_elk "Irish elk"), the [great auk](/wiki/Great_auk "Great auk"), [brown bear](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear") and the [wolf](/wiki/Wolves_in_Ireland "Wolves in Ireland"). Some previously extinct birds, such as the [golden eagle](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle"), have been reintroduced after decades of [extirpation](/wiki/Local_extinction "Local extinction").[[134]](#cite_note-137)

Ireland is now one of the least forested countries in Europe.[[135]](#cite_note-138)[[136]](#cite_note-139) Until the end of the Middle Ages, Ireland was heavily forested. Native species include [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees such as [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [ash](/wiki/Fraxinus_excelsior "Fraxinus excelsior"), [hazel](/wiki/Corylus_avellana "Corylus avellana"), [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch"), [alder](/wiki/Alnus_glutinosa "Alnus glutinosa"), [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow"), [aspen](/wiki/Populus_tremula "Populus tremula"), [rowan](/wiki/Sorbus_aucuparia "Sorbus aucuparia") and [hawthorn](/wiki/Crataegus_monogyna "Crataegus monogyna"), as well as [evergreen](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen") trees such [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine"), [yew](/wiki/Taxus_baccata "Taxus baccata"), [holly](/wiki/Ilex_aquifolium "Ilex aquifolium") and [strawberry trees](/wiki/Arbutus_unedo "Arbutus unedo").[[137]](#cite_note-140) Only about 10% of Ireland today is woodland;[[12]](#cite_note-forest-14) most of this is non-native conifer [plantations](/wiki/Plantation "Plantation"), and only 2% is native woodland.[[13]](#cite_note-forest2-15)[[14]](#cite_note-forest3-16) The average woodland cover of European countries is over 33%.[[12]](#cite_note-forest-14) In the Republic, about 389,356 hectares (3,893.56 km2) is owned by the state, mainly by the forestry service [Coillte](/wiki/Coillte "Coillte").[[12]](#cite_note-forest-14) Remnants of native forest can be found scattered around the island, in particular in the [Killarney National Park](/wiki/Killarney_National_Park "Killarney National Park").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Gorse-Ulex_europaeus.jpg/250px-Gorse-Ulex_europaeus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gorse-Ulex_europaeus.jpg)

Furze (*[Ulex europaeus](/wiki/Ulex_europaeus "Ulex europaeus")*)

Much of the land is now covered with pasture and there are many species of wild-flower. Gorse (*[Ulex europaeus](/wiki/Ulex_europaeus "Ulex europaeus")*), a wild [furze](/wiki/Furze "Furze"), is commonly found growing in the uplands and ferns are plentiful in the more moist regions, especially in the western parts. It is home to hundreds of plant species, some of them unique to the island, and has been "invaded" by some grasses, such as *[Spartina anglica](/wiki/Spartina_anglica "Spartina anglica")*.[[138]](#cite_note-141)

The algal and seaweed flora is that of the cold-temperate variety. The total number of species is 574[[139]](#cite_note-Guiry_and_Nic_Dhonncha_01-142) The island has been invaded by some algae, some of which are now well established.[[140]](#cite_note-Minchin_01-143)

Because of its mild climate, many species, including [sub-tropical](/wiki/Subtropics "Subtropics") species such as [palm trees](/wiki/Arecaceae "Arecaceae"), are grown in Ireland. [Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Ireland belongs to the Atlantic European province of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). The island can be subdivided into two [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"): the Celtic broadleaf forests and North Atlantic moist mixed forests.

### Impact of agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Silage_Harvesting_Cadamstown.jpg/250px-Silage_Harvesting_Cadamstown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silage_Harvesting_Cadamstown.jpg)

[Silage](/wiki/Silage "Silage") harvesting in Clonard, [County Meath](/wiki/County_Meath "County Meath")

The long history of agricultural production, coupled with modern intensive agricultural methods such as pesticide and fertiliser use and runoff from contaminants into streams, rivers and lakes, has placed pressure on biodiversity in Ireland.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145) A land of green fields for crop cultivation and cattle rearing limits the space available for the establishment of native wild species. Hedgerows, however, traditionally used for maintaining and demarcating land boundaries, act as a refuge for native wild flora. This ecosystem stretches across the countryside and acts as a network of connections to preserve remnants of the ecosystem that once covered the island. Subsidies under the [Common Agricultural Policy](/wiki/Common_Agricultural_Policy "Common Agricultural Policy"), which supported agricultural practices that preserved hedgerow environments, are undergoing reforms. The Common Agricultural Policy had in the past subsidised potentially destructive agricultural practices, for example by emphasising production without placing limits on indiscriminate use of fertilisers and pesticides; but reforms have gradually decoupled subsidies from production levels and introduced environmental and other requirements.[[143]](#cite_note-cap_reforms-146) 32% of Ireland's greenhouse gas emissions are correlated to agriculture.[[144]](#cite_note-147) Forested areas typically consist of monoculture plantations of non-native species, which may result in habitats that are not suitable for supporting native species of invertebrates. Natural areas require fencing to prevent over-grazing by [deer](/wiki/Deer_of_Ireland "Deer of Ireland") and sheep that roam over uncultivated areas. Grazing in this manner is one of the main factors preventing the natural regeneration of forests across many regions of the country.[[145]](#cite_note-148)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Irish people](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"), [Demographics of the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Demographics of the Republic of Ireland"), and [Demographics of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Northern_Ireland "Demographics of Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Catholicism_in_Ireland.png/250px-Catholicism_in_Ireland.png)](/wiki/File:Catholicism_in_Ireland.png)

Proportion of respondents to the Ireland census 2011 or the Northern Ireland census 2011 who stated they were Catholic. Areas in which Catholics are in the majority are blue. Areas in which Catholics are in a minority are red.

The population of Ireland is just over 7 million, of which approximately 5.1 million reside in the Republic of Ireland and 1.9 million reside in Northern Ireland.[[11]](#cite_note-2022population-13)

People have lived in Ireland for over 9,000 years. Early historical and genealogical records note the existence of major groups such as the *[Cruthin](/wiki/Cruthin "Cruthin")*, *[Corcu Loígde](/wiki/Corcu_Lo%C3%ADgde "Corcu Loígde")*, *[Dál Riata](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_Riata "Dál Riata")*, *[Dáirine](/wiki/D%C3%A1irine "Dáirine")*, *[Deirgtine](/wiki/Deirgtine "Deirgtine")*, *[Delbhna](/wiki/Delbhna "Delbhna")*, *[Érainn](/wiki/%C3%89rainn "Érainn")*, *[Laigin](/wiki/Laigin "Laigin")*, *[Ulaid](/wiki/Ulaid "Ulaid")*. Later major groups included the *[Connachta](/wiki/Connachta "Connachta")*, *[Ciannachta](/wiki/Ciannachta "Ciannachta")*, *[Eóganachta](/wiki/E%C3%B3ganachta "Eóganachta")*. Smaller groups included the *aithechthúatha* (see *[Attacotti](/wiki/Attacotti "Attacotti")*), *[Cálraighe](/wiki/C%C3%A1lraighe "Cálraighe")*, *[Cíarraige](/wiki/C%C3%ADarraige "Cíarraige")*, *[Conmaicne](/wiki/Conmaicne "Conmaicne")*, *[Dartraighe](/wiki/Dartraighe "Dartraighe")*, *[Déisi](/wiki/D%C3%A9isi "Déisi")*, *[Éile](/wiki/%C3%89ile "Éile")*, *[Fir Bolg](/wiki/Fir_Bolg "Fir Bolg")*, *[Fortuatha](/wiki/Fortuatha "Fortuatha")*, *[Gailenga](/wiki/Gailenga "Gailenga")*, *[Gamanraige](/wiki/Gamanraige "Gamanraige")*, *[Mairtine](/wiki/Mairtine "Mairtine")*, *[Múscraige](/wiki/M%C3%BAscraige "Múscraige")*, *[Partraige](/wiki/Partraige "Partraige")*, *[Soghain](/wiki/Soghain "Soghain")*, *[Uaithni](/wiki/Uaithni "Uaithni")*, *[Uí Maine](/wiki/U%C3%AD_Maine "Uí Maine")*, *[Uí Liatháin](/wiki/U%C3%AD_Liath%C3%A1in "Uí Liatháin")*. Many survived into late medieval times, others vanished as they became politically unimportant. Over the past 1,200 years, [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings"), [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), [Flemings](/wiki/Flemings "Flemings"), [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), English, [Africans](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora") and Eastern Europeans have all added to the population and have had significant influences on Irish culture.

The population of Ireland rose rapidly from the 16th century until the mid-19th century, interrupted briefly by the [Famine of 1740–41](/wiki/Irish_Famine_(1740%E2%80%9341) "Irish Famine (1740–41)"), which killed roughly two-fifths of the island's population. The population rebounded and multiplied over the next century, but the Great Famine of the 1840s caused one million deaths and forced over one million more to emigrate in its immediate wake. Over the following century, the population was reduced by over half, at a time when the general trend in European countries was for populations to rise by an average of three-fold.

Ireland's largest religious group is Christianity. The largest denomination is [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism"), representing over 73% of the island (and about 87% of the Republic of Ireland). Most of the rest of the population adhere to one of the various [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") denominations (about 48% of Northern Ireland).[[146]](#cite_note-niprotestants-149) The largest is the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"). The [Muslim community](/wiki/Islam_in_Ireland "Islam in Ireland") is growing in Ireland, mostly through increased immigration, with a 50% increase in the republic between the 2006 and 2011 census.[[147]](#cite_note-150) The island has a small [Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Ireland "History of the Jews in Ireland"). About 4% of the Republic's population and about 14% of the Northern Ireland population[[146]](#cite_note-niprotestants-149) describe themselves as of no religion. In a 2010 survey conducted on behalf of the [Irish Times](/wiki/Irish_Times "Irish Times"), 32% of respondents said they went to a religious service more than once per week.

[Provinces of Ireland](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland")

Traditionally, Ireland is subdivided into [four provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland"): [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht "Connacht") (west), [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster "Leinster") (east), [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster") (south), and [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") (north). In a system that developed between the 13th and 17th centuries,[[148]](#cite_note-151) Ireland has [32 traditional counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland"). Twenty-six of these counties are in the Republic of Ireland, and [six are in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Counties_of_Northern_Ireland "Counties of Northern Ireland"). The six counties that constitute Northern Ireland are all in the province of Ulster (which has nine counties in total). As such, *Ulster* is often used as a synonym for Northern Ireland, although the two are not coterminous. In the Republic of Ireland, counties form the basis of the system of local government. Counties [Dublin](/wiki/County_Dublin "County Dublin"), [Cork](/wiki/County_Cork "County Cork"), [Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"), [Galway](/wiki/County_Galway "County Galway"), [Waterford](/wiki/County_Waterford "County Waterford") and [Tipperary](/wiki/County_Tipperary "County Tipperary") have been broken up into smaller administrative areas. However, they are still treated as counties for cultural and some official purposes, for example, postal addresses and by the [Ordnance Survey Ireland](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_Ireland "Ordnance Survey Ireland"). Counties in Northern Ireland are [no longer used](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland") for local governmental purposes,[[149]](#cite_note-152) but, as in the Republic, their traditional boundaries are still used for informal purposes such as sports leagues and in cultural or tourism contexts.[[150]](#cite_note-153)

City status in Ireland is decided by [legislative](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation") or [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"). Dublin, with over one million residents in the [Greater Dublin Area](/wiki/Greater_Dublin_Area "Greater Dublin Area"), is the largest city on the island. Belfast, with 579,726 residents, is the largest city in Northern Ireland. City status does not directly equate with population size. For example, [Armagh](/wiki/Armagh "Armagh"), with 14,590 is the seat of the Church of Ireland and the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [Primate of All Ireland](/wiki/Primate_of_All_Ireland "Primate of All Ireland") and was re-granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom#Northern_Ireland "City status in the United Kingdom") by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") in 1994 (having lost that status in [local government reforms of 1840](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_(Ireland)_Act_1840 "Municipal Corporations (Ireland) Act 1840")). In the Republic of Ireland, [Kilkenny](/wiki/Kilkenny "Kilkenny"), the seat of the [Butler dynasty](/wiki/Butler_dynasty "Butler dynasty"), while no longer a city for administrative purposes (since the 2001 [Local Government Act](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_2001 "Local Government Act 2001")), is entitled by law to continue to use the description.

### Migration

The population of Ireland collapsed dramatically during the second half of the 19th century. A population of over eight million in 1841 was reduced to slightly over four million by 1921. In part, the fall in population was caused by death from the Great Famine of 1845 to 1852, which took roughly one million lives. The remaining decline of around three million was due to the entrenched culture of emigration caused by the dire economic state of the country, lasting until the late 20th century.

Emigration from Ireland in the 19th century contributed to the populations of England, the United States, Canada and Australia, in all of which a large [Irish diaspora](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") lives. As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ireland&action=edit), 4.3 million Canadians, or 14% of the population, were of Irish descent,[[153]](#cite_note-157) while around one-third of the Australian population had an element of Irish descent.[[154]](#cite_note-SMH_Irish-158) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ireland&action=edit), there were 40 million Irish-Americans[[155]](#cite_note-159) and 33 million Americans who claimed Irish ancestry.[[156]](#cite_note-160)

With growing prosperity since the last decade of the 20th century, Ireland became a destination for immigrants. Since the European Union expanded to include Poland in 2004, [Polish people](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") have comprised the largest number of immigrants (over 150,000)[[157]](#cite_note-161) from Central Europe. There has also been significant immigration from Lithuania, Czech Republic and Latvia.[[158]](#cite_note-162)

[The Republic of Ireland in particular](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Republic_of_Ireland#Immigration "Demographics of the Republic of Ireland") has seen large-scale immigration, with 420,000 foreign nationals as of 2006, about 10% of the population.[[159]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-163) Nearly a quarter of births (24 percent) in 2009 were to mothers born outside of Ireland.[[160]](#cite_note-164) Up to 50,000 eastern and central European migrant workers left Ireland in response to the post-2008 Irish economic downturn.[[161]](#cite_note-affluenceends-165)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Ireland](/wiki/Languages_of_Ireland "Languages of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Irish_speakers_in_2011.png/330px-Irish_speakers_in_2011.png)](/wiki/File:Irish_speakers_in_2011.png)

Proportion of respondents who said they could speak Irish in the Ireland census in 2011 or the Northern Ireland census in 2011

The two official languages of the Republic of Ireland are Irish and English. Each language has produced noteworthy literature. Irish, though now only the language of a minority, was the vernacular of the Irish people for thousands of years and was possibly introduced during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"). It began to be written down after Christianisation in the 5th century and spread to Scotland and the Isle of Man, where it evolved into the [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") and [Manx](/wiki/Manx_Language "Manx Language") languages, respectively.

The Irish language has a vast treasury of written texts from many centuries and is divided by linguists into [Old Irish](/wiki/Old_Irish "Old Irish") from the 6th to 10th century, [Middle Irish](/wiki/Middle_Irish "Middle Irish") from the 10th to 13th century, Early Modern Irish until the 17th century, and the Modern Irish spoken today. It remained the dominant language of Ireland for most of those periods, having influences from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_Language "Latin Language"), [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"), [French](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") and English. It declined under British rule but remained the majority tongue until the early 19th century, and since then has been a minority language.

The [Gaelic Revival](/wiki/Gaelic_Revival "Gaelic Revival") of the late 19th and early 20th centuries had a long-term influence. Irish is taught in mainstream Irish schools as a compulsory subject, but teaching methods have been criticised for their ineffectiveness, with most students showing little evidence of fluency even after 14 years of instruction.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

There is now a growing population of urban Irish speakers in both the Republic and Northern Ireland, especially in Dublin[[163]](#cite_note-schism-167)[[164]](#cite_note-168) and Belfast,[[165]](#cite_note-169) with the children of such Irish speakers sometimes attending Irish-medium schools (*[Gaelscoil](/wiki/Gaelscoil "Gaelscoil") or Gaelscoileanna*). It has been argued that they tend to be more highly educated than monolingual English speakers.[[166]](#cite_note-cso.ie-170) Recent research suggests that urban Irish is developing in a direction of its own, both in pronunciation and grammar.[[167]](#cite_note-irishtimes.com-171)

Traditional rural Irish-speaking areas, known collectively as the *[Gaeltacht](/wiki/Gaeltacht "Gaeltacht")*, are in linguistic decline. The main *Gaeltacht* areas are in the west, south-west and north-west, in Galway, Mayo, Donegal, western Cork and Kerry with smaller *Gaeltacht* areas near [Dungarvan](/wiki/Dungarvan "Dungarvan") in Waterford and in Meath.[[168]](#cite_note-172)

[English in Ireland](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English") was first introduced during the Norman invasion. It was spoken by a few peasants and merchants brought over from England and was largely replaced by Irish before the Tudor conquest of Ireland. It was introduced as the official language during the Tudor and Cromwellian conquests. The Ulster plantations gave it a permanent foothold in Ulster, and it remained the official and upper-class language elsewhere, the Irish-speaking chieftains and nobility having been deposed. Language shift during the 19th century replaced Irish with English as the first language for a vast majority of the population.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

Fewer than 2% of the population of the Republic of Ireland today speak Irish on a daily basis, and under 10% regularly, outside of the education system[[170]](#cite_note-174) and 38% of those over 15 years are classified as "Irish speakers". In Northern Ireland, English is the de facto official language, but official recognition is afforded to Irish, including specific protective measures under Part III of the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"). A lesser status (including recognition under Part II of the Charter) is given to [Ulster Scots dialects](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects"), which are spoken by roughly 2% of Northern Ireland residents, and also spoken by some in the Republic of Ireland.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Since the 1960s with the increase in immigration, many more languages have been introduced, particularly deriving from Asia and Eastern Europe.

Also native to Ireland are [Shelta](/wiki/Shelta_language "Shelta language"), the language of the nomadic [Irish Travellers](/wiki/Irish_Travellers "Irish Travellers"),[[172]](#cite_note-McArthur-176) [Irish Sign Language](/wiki/Irish_Sign_Language "Irish Sign Language"), and [Northern Ireland Sign Language](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Sign_Language "Northern Ireland Sign Language").

## Culture

Main articles: [Culture of Ireland](/wiki/Culture_of_Ireland "Culture of Ireland") and [Culture of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Culture_of_Northern_Ireland "Culture of Northern Ireland")

[![Tall stone cross, with intricate carved patterns, protected by metal railings surrounded by short cut grass. Trees are to either side, cows in open countryside are in the middle distance.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Ardboe_Cross.jpg/250px-Ardboe_Cross.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ardboe_Cross.jpg)

[Ardboe High Cross](/wiki/Ardboe_High_Cross "Ardboe High Cross"), County Tyrone

Ireland's culture comprises elements of the culture of ancient peoples, later immigrant and broadcast cultural influences (chiefly Gaelic culture, [Anglicisation](/wiki/Anglicisation "Anglicisation"), [Americanisation](/wiki/Americanisation "Americanisation") and aspects of broader [European culture](/wiki/European_culture "European culture")). In broad terms, Ireland is regarded as one of the [Celtic nations](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations") of Europe, alongside Scotland, Wales, Cornwall, [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") and Brittany. This combination of cultural influences is visible in the intricate designs termed *Irish [interlace](/wiki/Interlace_(visual_arts) "Interlace (visual arts)")* or *[Celtic knotwork](/wiki/Celtic_knot "Celtic knot").* These can be seen in the ornamentation of medieval religious and secular works. The style is still popular today in jewellery and graphic art,[[173]](#cite_note-BBC_1-177) as is the distinctive style of [traditional Irish music](/wiki/Traditional_Irish_music "Traditional Irish music") and dance, and has become indicative of modern "Celtic" culture in general.

[Religion](/wiki/Religion_in_Ireland "Religion in Ireland") has played a significant role in the cultural life of the island since ancient times (and since the 17th century plantations, has been the focus of political identity and divisions on the island). Ireland's pre-Christian heritage fused with the Celtic Church following the missions of Saint Patrick in the fifth century. The Hiberno-Scottish missions, begun by the Irish monk Saint Columba, spread the Irish vision of Christianity to [pagan](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") England and the Frankish Empire. These missions brought written language to an illiterate population of Europe during the Dark Ages that followed the [fall of Rome](/wiki/Fall_of_Rome "Fall of Rome"), earning Ireland the sobriquet, "the island of saints and scholars".

Since the 20th century [Irish pubs](/wiki/Irish_pub "Irish pub") worldwide have become outposts of Irish culture, especially those with a full range of cultural and gastronomic offerings.

### Arts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg/250px-KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)](/wiki/File:KellsFol032vChristEnthroned.jpg)

Illuminated page from [Book of Kells](/wiki/Book_of_Kells "Book of Kells")

#### Literature

Main article: [Literature of Ireland](/wiki/Literature_of_Ireland "Literature of Ireland")

Ireland has made a substantial contribution to world literature in all its branches, both in Irish and English. Poetry in Irish is among the oldest [vernacular poetry](/wiki/Vernacular_literature "Vernacular literature") in Europe, with the earliest examples dating from the 6th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
Irish remained the dominant literary language down to the 19th century, despite the spread of English from the 17th century on. Prominent names from the medieval period and later include [Gofraidh Fionn Ó Dálaigh](/wiki/Gofraidh_Fionn_%C3%93_D%C3%A1laigh "Gofraidh Fionn Ó Dálaigh") (14th century), [Dáibhí Ó Bruadair](/wiki/D%C3%A1ibh%C3%AD_%C3%93_Bruadair "Dáibhí Ó Bruadair") (17th century) and [Aogán Ó Rathaille](/wiki/Aog%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Rathaille "Aogán Ó Rathaille") (18th century). [Eibhlín Dubh Ní Chonaill](/wiki/Eibhl%C3%ADn_Dubh_N%C3%AD_Chonaill "Eibhlín Dubh Ní Chonaill") (c. 1743 – c. 1800) was an outstanding poet in the oral tradition. The latter part of the 19th century saw a rapid replacement of Irish by English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By 1900, however, cultural nationalists had begun the [Gaelic revival](/wiki/Gaelic_revival "Gaelic revival"), which saw the beginnings of modern literature in Irish. This was to produce a number of notable writers, including [Máirtín Ó Cadhain](/wiki/M%C3%A1irt%C3%ADn_%C3%93_Cadhain "Máirtín Ó Cadhain"), [Máire Mhac an tSaoi](/wiki/M%C3%A1ire_Mhac_an_tSaoi "Máire Mhac an tSaoi") and others. Irish-language publishers such as [Coiscéim](/wiki/Coisc%C3%A9im "Coiscéim") and [Cló Iar-Chonnacht](/wiki/Cl%C3%B3_Iar-Chonnacht "Cló Iar-Chonnacht") continue to produce scores of titles every year.

In English, [Jonathan Swift](/wiki/Jonathan_Swift "Jonathan Swift"), often called the foremost satirist in the English language, gained fame for works such as *[Gulliver's Travels](/wiki/Gulliver%27s_Travels "Gulliver's Travels")* and *[A Modest Proposal](/wiki/A_Modest_Proposal "A Modest Proposal")*. Other notable 18th-century writers of Irish origin included [Oliver Goldsmith](/wiki/Oliver_Goldsmith "Oliver Goldsmith") and [Richard Brinsley Sheridan](/wiki/Richard_Brinsley_Sheridan "Richard Brinsley Sheridan"), though they spent most of their lives in England. The Anglo-Irish novel came to the fore in the 19th century, featuring such writers as [Charles Kickham](/wiki/Charles_Kickham "Charles Kickham"), [William Carleton](/wiki/William_Carleton "William Carleton"), and (in collaboration) [Edith Somerville](/wiki/Edith_Somerville "Edith Somerville") and [Violet Florence Martin](/wiki/Violet_Florence_Martin "Violet Florence Martin"). The playwright and poet [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde"), noted for his epigrams, was born in Ireland.

In the 20th century, Ireland produced four winners of the [Nobel Prize for Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_for_Literature "Nobel Prize for Literature"): [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw"), [William Butler Yeats](/wiki/William_Butler_Yeats "William Butler Yeats"), [Samuel Beckett](/wiki/Samuel_Beckett "Samuel Beckett") and [Seamus Heaney](/wiki/Seamus_Heaney "Seamus Heaney"). Although not a Nobel Prize winner, [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce") is widely considered to be one of the most significant writers of the 20th century. Joyce's 1922 novel *[Ulysses](/wiki/Ulysses_(novel) "Ulysses (novel)")* is considered one of the most important works of [Modernist literature](/wiki/Modernist_literature "Modernist literature") and his life is celebrated annually on 16 June in Dublin as "[Bloomsday](/wiki/Bloomsday "Bloomsday")".[[174]](#cite_note-178) A comparable writer in Irish is [Máirtín Ó Cadhain](/wiki/M%C3%A1irt%C3%ADn_%C3%93_Cadhain "Máirtín Ó Cadhain"), whose 1949 novel *[Cré na Cille](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9_na_Cille "Cré na Cille")* is regarded as a modernist masterpiece and has been translated into several languages.

Modern Irish literature is often connected with its rural heritage[[175]](#cite_note-179) through English-language writers such as [John McGahern](/wiki/John_McGahern "John McGahern") and Seamus Heaney and Irish-language writers such as [Máirtín Ó Direáin](/wiki/M%C3%A1irt%C3%ADn_%C3%93_Dire%C3%A1in "Máirtín Ó Direáin") and others from the Gaeltacht.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Revolutionary_Joyce.jpg/250px-Revolutionary_Joyce.jpg)](/wiki/File:Revolutionary_Joyce.jpg)

[James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce"), one of the most significant writers of the 20th century

#### Music and dance

Main articles: [Music of Ireland](/wiki/Music_of_Ireland "Music of Ireland") and [Irish dance](/wiki/Irish_dance "Irish dance")

Music has been in evidence in Ireland since prehistoric times.[[176]](#cite_note-180) Although in the early Middle Ages the church was "quite unlike its counterpart in continental Europe",[[177]](#cite_note-181) there was a considerable interchange between monastic settlements in Ireland and the rest of Europe that contributed to what is known as [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant"). Outside religious establishments, musical genres in early Gaelic Ireland are referred to as a triad of weeping music (*goltraige*), laughing music (*geantraige*) and sleeping music (*suantraige*).[[178]](#cite_note-182) Vocal and instrumental music (e.g. for the harp, pipes, and various [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument")) was transmitted orally, but the [Irish harp](/wiki/Irish_harp "Irish harp"), in particular, was of such significance that it became Ireland's national symbol. Classical music following European models first developed in urban areas, in establishments of Anglo-Irish rule such as [Dublin Castle](/wiki/Dublin_Castle "Dublin Castle"), [St Patrick's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Cathedral,_Dublin "St Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin") and [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral,_Dublin "Christ Church Cathedral, Dublin") as well as the country houses of the Anglo-Irish ascendancy, with the first performance of [Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel")'s *[Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)")* (1742) being among the highlights of the baroque era. In the 19th century, public concerts provided access to classical music to all classes of society. Yet, for political and financial reasons Ireland has been too small to provide a living to many musicians, so the names of the better-known [Irish composers](/wiki/List_of_Irish_classical_composers "List of Irish classical composers") of this time belong to emigrants.

Irish [traditional music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and dance have seen a surge in popularity and global coverage since the 1960s. In the middle years of the 20th century, as Irish society was modernising, traditional music had fallen out of favour, especially in urban areas.[[179]](#cite_note-183) However during the 1960s, there was a revival of interest in Irish traditional music led by groups such as [the Dubliners](/wiki/The_Dubliners "The Dubliners"), [the Chieftains](/wiki/The_Chieftains "The Chieftains"), [the Wolfe Tones](/wiki/The_Wolfe_Tones "The Wolfe Tones"), the [Clancy Brothers](/wiki/Clancy_Brothers "Clancy Brothers"), [Sweeney's Men](/wiki/Sweeney%27s_Men "Sweeney's Men") and individuals like [Seán Ó Riada](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Riada "Seán Ó Riada") and [Christy Moore](/wiki/Christy_Moore "Christy Moore"). Groups and musicians including [Horslips](/wiki/Horslips "Horslips"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison") and [Thin Lizzy](/wiki/Thin_Lizzy "Thin Lizzy") incorporated elements of Irish traditional music into contemporary rock music and, during the 1970s and 1980s, the distinction between traditional and rock musicians became blurred, with many individuals regularly crossing over between these styles of playing. This trend can be seen more recently in the work of artists like [Enya](/wiki/Enya "Enya"), [the Saw Doctors](/wiki/The_Saw_Doctors "The Saw Doctors"), [the Corrs](/wiki/The_Corrs "The Corrs"), [Sinéad O'Connor](/wiki/Sin%C3%A9ad_O%27Connor "Sinéad O'Connor"), [Clannad](/wiki/Clannad_(musical_group) "Clannad (musical group)"), [the Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries") and [the Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues") among others.

#### Art

Main articles: [Art of Ireland](/wiki/Art_of_Ireland "Art of Ireland") and [Architecture of Ireland](/wiki/Architecture_of_Ireland "Architecture of Ireland")

The earliest known Irish graphic art and sculpture are Neolithic carvings found at sites such as Newgrange[[180]](#cite_note-184) and is traced through Bronze Age artefacts and the religious carvings and [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscripts "Illuminated manuscripts") of the medieval period. During the course of the 19th and 20th centuries, a strong tradition of painting emerged, including such figures as [John Butler Yeats](/wiki/John_Butler_Yeats "John Butler Yeats"), [William Orpen](/wiki/William_Orpen "William Orpen"), [Jack Yeats](/wiki/Jack_Yeats "Jack Yeats") and [Louis le Brocquy](/wiki/Louis_le_Brocquy "Louis le Brocquy"). Contemporary Irish visual artists of note include [Sean Scully](/wiki/Sean_Scully "Sean Scully"), [Kevin Abosch](/wiki/Kevin_Abosch "Kevin Abosch"), and [Alice Maher](/wiki/Alice_Maher "Alice Maher").

#### Drama and theatre

Main article: [Irish theatre](/wiki/Irish_theatre "Irish theatre")

The Republic of Ireland's national theatre is the [Abbey Theatre](/wiki/Abbey_Theatre "Abbey Theatre"), which was founded in 1904, and the national Irish-language theatre is *[An Taibhdhearc](/wiki/An_Taibhdhearc "An Taibhdhearc")*, which was established in 1928 in [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway").[[181]](#cite_note-185)[[182]](#cite_note-186) Playwrights such as [Seán O'Casey](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_O%27Casey "Seán O'Casey"), [Brian Friel](/wiki/Brian_Friel "Brian Friel"), [Sebastian Barry](/wiki/Sebastian_Barry "Sebastian Barry"), [Conor McPherson](/wiki/Conor_McPherson "Conor McPherson") and [Billy Roche](/wiki/Billy_Roche "Billy Roche") are internationally renowned.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

### Science

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/The_Shannon_Portrait_of_the_Hon_Robert_Boyle.jpg/250px-The_Shannon_Portrait_of_the_Hon_Robert_Boyle.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Shannon_Portrait_of_the_Hon_Robert_Boyle.jpg)

[Robert Boyle](/wiki/Robert_Boyle "Robert Boyle") formulated Boyle's Law.

The Irish philosopher and theologian [Johannes Scotus Eriugena](/wiki/Johannes_Scotus_Eriugena "Johannes Scotus Eriugena") was considered one of the leading intellectuals of the early Middle Ages. Sir [Ernest Henry Shackleton](/wiki/Ernest_Henry_Shackleton "Ernest Henry Shackleton"), an Irish explorer, was one of the principal figures of Antarctic exploration. He, along with his expedition, made the first ascent of [Mount Erebus](/wiki/Mount_Erebus "Mount Erebus") and the discovery of the approximate location of the [South Magnetic Pole](/wiki/South_Magnetic_Pole "South Magnetic Pole"). [Robert Boyle](/wiki/Robert_Boyle "Robert Boyle") was a 17th-century natural philosopher, chemist, physicist, inventor and early [gentleman scientist](/wiki/Gentleman_scientist "Gentleman scientist"). He is largely regarded as one of the founders of modern chemistry and is best known for the formulation of [Boyle's law](/wiki/Boyle%27s_law "Boyle's law").[[184]](#cite_note-ucc_boyle_boi-188)

19th-century physicist, [John Tyndall](/wiki/John_Tyndall "John Tyndall"), discovered the [Tyndall effect](/wiki/Tyndall_effect "Tyndall effect"). [Father Nicholas Joseph Callan](/wiki/Father_Nicholas_Joseph_Callan "Father Nicholas Joseph Callan"), professor of natural philosophy in [Maynooth College](/wiki/Maynooth_College "Maynooth College"), is best known for his invention of the [induction coil](/wiki/Induction_coil "Induction coil"), [transformer](/wiki/Transformer "Transformer") and he discovered an early method of [galvanisation](/wiki/Galvanisation "Galvanisation") in the 19th century.

Other notable Irish [physicists](/wiki/Physicists "Physicists") include [Ernest Walton](/wiki/Ernest_Walton "Ernest Walton"), winner of the 1951 [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"). With [Sir John Douglas Cockcroft](/wiki/Sir_John_Douglas_Cockcroft "Sir John Douglas Cockcroft"), he was the first to split the nucleus of the atom by artificial means and made contributions to the development of a new theory of [wave equation](/wiki/Wave_equation "Wave equation").[[185]](#cite_note-189) William Thomson, or [Lord Kelvin](/wiki/Lord_Kelvin "Lord Kelvin"), is the person whom the absolute temperature unit, the [kelvin](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin"), is named after. Sir [Joseph Larmor](/wiki/Joseph_Larmor "Joseph Larmor"), a physicist and mathematician, made innovations in the understanding of electricity, dynamics, thermodynamics and the electron theory of matter. His most influential work was Aether and Matter, a book on theoretical physics published in 1900.[[186]](#cite_note-physicsworld-190)

[George Johnstone Stoney](/wiki/George_Johnstone_Stoney "George Johnstone Stoney") introduced the term *[electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron")* in 1891. [John Stewart Bell](/wiki/John_Stewart_Bell "John Stewart Bell") was the originator of [Bell's Theorem](/wiki/Bell%27s_Theorem "Bell's Theorem") and a paper concerning the discovery of the [Bell-Jackiw-Adler anomaly](/wiki/Chiral_anomaly "Chiral anomaly") and was nominated for a Nobel prize.[[187]](#cite_note-191) The astronomer [Jocelyn Bell Burnell](/wiki/Jocelyn_Bell_Burnell "Jocelyn Bell Burnell"), from [Lurgan](/wiki/Lurgan "Lurgan"), County Armagh, discovered pulsars in 1967. Notable mathematicians include Sir [William Rowan Hamilton](/wiki/William_Rowan_Hamilton "William Rowan Hamilton"), famous for work in [classical mechanics](/wiki/Hamiltonian_mechanics "Hamiltonian mechanics") and the invention of [quaternions](/wiki/Quaternions "Quaternions"). [Francis Ysidro Edgeworth](/wiki/Francis_Ysidro_Edgeworth "Francis Ysidro Edgeworth")'s contribution, the [Edgeworth Box](/wiki/Edgeworth_Box "Edgeworth Box"). remains influential in neo-classical microeconomic theory to this day; while [Richard Cantillon](/wiki/Richard_Cantillon "Richard Cantillon") inspired [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith"), among others. [John B. Cosgrave](/wiki/John_B._Cosgrave "John B. Cosgrave") was a specialist in [number theory](/wiki/Number_theory "Number theory") and discovered a 2000-digit [prime number](/wiki/Prime_number "Prime number") in 1999 and a record composite [Fermat number](/wiki/Fermat_number "Fermat number") in 2003. [John Lighton Synge](/wiki/John_Lighton_Synge "John Lighton Synge") made progress in different fields of science, including mechanics and geometrical methods in general relativity. He had mathematician [John Nash](/wiki/John_Forbes_Nash_Jr. "John Forbes Nash Jr.") as one of his students. [Kathleen Lonsdale](/wiki/Kathleen_Lonsdale "Kathleen Lonsdale"), born in Ireland and most known for her work with [crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography"), became the first female president of the British Association for the Advancement of Science.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

Ireland has nine universities, seven in the Republic of Ireland and two in Northern Ireland, including [Trinity College Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin") and the [University College Dublin](/wiki/University_College_Dublin "University College Dublin"), as well as numerous third-level colleges and institutes and a branch of the Open University, the [Open University in Ireland](/wiki/Open_University_in_Ireland "Open University in Ireland"). Ireland was ranked 18th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[190]](#cite_note-194)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Ireland](/wiki/Sport_in_Ireland "Sport in Ireland")

See also: [List of Irish sports people](/wiki/List_of_Irish_people#Sport "List of Irish people")

[Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football") is the most popular sport in Ireland in terms of match attendance and community involvement, with about 2,600 clubs on the island. In 2003 it represented 34% of total sports attendances at events in Ireland and abroad, followed by [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") at 23%, soccer at 16% and [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") at 8%.[[191]](#cite_note-esri-195) The [All-Ireland Football Final](/wiki/All-Ireland_Football_Final "All-Ireland Football Final") is the most watched event in the sporting calendar.[[192]](#cite_note-196) Soccer is the most widely played team game on the island and the most popular in Northern Ireland.[[191]](#cite_note-esri-195)[[193]](#cite_note-FootballSoccer-197)

Other sporting activities with the highest levels of playing participation include swimming, golf, aerobics, cycling, and billiards/snooker.[[194]](#cite_note-198) Many other sports are also played and followed, including [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), fishing, [greyhound racing](/wiki/Greyhound_racing "Greyhound racing"), [handball](/wiki/Gaelic_handball "Gaelic handball"), [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), horse racing, [motor sport](/wiki/Motor_sport "Motor sport"), [show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping") and tennis.

The island fields a single international team in most sports. One notable exception to this is association football, although both associations continued to field international teams under the name "Ireland" until the 1950s. The sport is also the most notable exception where the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") field separate international teams. Northern Ireland has produced two World Snooker Champions.

Ireland is ranked 10th in the ICC Men's Test Team Rankings 2025 with a rating of 30.[[195]](#cite_note-199)

#### Field sports

Main articles: [Gaelic games](/wiki/Gaelic_games "Gaelic games"), [Rugby union in Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Ireland "Rugby union in Ireland"), [Rugby league in Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_Ireland "Rugby league in Ireland"), [Association football in the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Association_football_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Association football in the Republic of Ireland"), and [Association football in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Association_football_in_Northern_Ireland "Association football in Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Tyrone_Blanket_Defence.jpg/250px-Tyrone_Blanket_Defence.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tyrone_Blanket_Defence.jpg)

[Tyrone](/wiki/Tyrone_GAA "Tyrone GAA") v [Kerry](/wiki/Kerry_GAA "Kerry GAA") in the [2005 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship Final](/wiki/2005_All-Ireland_Senior_Football_Championship_Final "2005 All-Ireland Senior Football Championship Final")

Gaelic football, hurling and Gaelic handball are the best-known Irish traditional sports, collectively known as Gaelic games. Gaelic games are governed by the [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") (GAA), with the exception of women's Gaelic football and camogie (women's variant of hurling), which are governed by separate organisations. The headquarters of the GAA (and the main stadium) is located at[[196]](#cite_note-200) [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park") in north Dublin and has a capacity of 82,500. Many major GAA games are played there, including the semi-finals and finals of the [All-Ireland Senior Football Championship](/wiki/All-Ireland_Senior_Football_Championship "All-Ireland Senior Football Championship") and [All-Ireland Senior Hurling Championship](/wiki/All-Ireland_Senior_Hurling_Championship "All-Ireland Senior Hurling Championship"). During the redevelopment of the [Lansdowne Road stadium](/wiki/Lansdowne_Road "Lansdowne Road") in 2007–2010, international rugby and soccer were played there.[[197]](#cite_note-201) All GAA players, even at the highest level, are amateurs, receiving no wages, although they are permitted to receive a limited amount of sport-related income from commercial sponsorship.

The [Irish Football Association](/wiki/Irish_Football_Association "Irish Football Association") (IFA) was originally the governing body for soccer across the island. The game has been played in an organised fashion in Ireland since the 1870s, with [Cliftonville F.C.](/wiki/Cliftonville_F.C. "Cliftonville F.C.") in Belfast being Ireland's oldest club. It was most popular, especially in its first decades, around Belfast and in Ulster. However, some clubs based outside Belfast thought that the IFA largely favoured Ulster-based clubs in such matters as selection for the national team. In 1921, following an incident in which, despite an earlier promise, the IFA moved an [Irish Cup](/wiki/Irish_Cup "Irish Cup") semi-final replay from Dublin to Belfast,[[198]](#cite_note-202) Dublin-based clubs broke away to form the Football Association of the Irish Free State. Today the southern association is known as the [Football Association of Ireland](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Ireland "Football Association of Ireland") (FAI). Despite being initially blacklisted by the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations")' associations, the FAI was recognised by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1923 and organised its first international fixture in 1926 (against [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team")). However, both the IFA and FAI continued to select their teams from the whole of Ireland, with some players earning international caps for matches with both teams. Both also referred to their respective teams as *Ireland*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Paul_O%27Connell_Ireland_Rugby.jpg/250px-Paul_O%27Connell_Ireland_Rugby.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_O%27Connell_Ireland_Rugby.jpg)

[Paul O'Connell](/wiki/Paul_O%27Connell "Paul O'Connell") reaching for the ball during a [line out](/wiki/Line-out_(rugby_union) "Line-out (rugby union)") against [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_rugby_union_team "Argentina national rugby union team") in 2007

In 1950, FIFA directed the associations only to select players from within their respective territories and, in 1953, directed that the FAI's team be known only as "Republic of Ireland" and that the IFA's team be known as "Northern Ireland" (with certain exceptions). Northern Ireland qualified for the [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") finals in [1958](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup "1958 FIFA World Cup") (reaching the quarter-finals), [1982](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup") and [1986](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup") and the [European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") in [2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"). The Republic qualified for the World Cup finals in [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") (reaching the quarter-finals), [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") and the European Championship in [1988](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1988 "UEFA Euro 1988"), [2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012") and 2016. Across Ireland, there is significant interest in the [English](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and, to a lesser extent, [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") soccer leagues.

Ireland fields a single [national rugby team](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team") and a single association, the [Irish Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union"), governs the sport across the island. The Irish rugby team have played in every [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), making the quarter-finals in eight of them.[[199]](#cite_note-203) Ireland also hosted games during the [1991](/wiki/1991_Rugby_World_Cup "1991 Rugby World Cup") and the [1999 Rugby World Cups](/wiki/1999_Rugby_World_Cup "1999 Rugby World Cup") (including a quarter-final). There are four professional Irish teams; all four play in the [Pro14](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14") and at least three compete for the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup"). Irish rugby has become increasingly competitive at both the international and provincial levels since the sport went professional in 1994. During that time, [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby") ([1999](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Heineken_Cup "1998–99 Heineken Cup")),[[200]](#cite_note-heineken_champions_archive-204) [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") ([2006](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Heineken_Cup "2005–06 Heineken Cup")[[201]](#cite_note-205) and [2008](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Heineken_Cup "2007–08 Heineken Cup"))[[200]](#cite_note-heineken_champions_archive-204) and [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") ([2009](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Heineken_Cup "2008–09 Heineken Cup"), [2011](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Heineken_Cup "2010–11 Heineken Cup") and [2012](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Heineken_Cup "2011–12 Heineken Cup"))[[200]](#cite_note-heineken_champions_archive-204) have won the Heineken Cup. In addition to this, the Irish International side has had increased success in the [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") against the other European elite sides. This success, including [Triple Crowns](/wiki/Triple_Crown_(rugby_union) "Triple Crown (rugby union)") in 2004, 2006 and 2007, culminated with a clean sweep of victories, known as a [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(rugby_union) "Grand Slam (rugby union)"), in 2009 and 2018.[[202]](#cite_note-206)

#### Boxing

Main article: [Boxing in Ireland](/wiki/Boxing_in_Ireland "Boxing in Ireland")

Amateur boxing on the island of Ireland is governed by the [Irish Athletic Boxing Association](/wiki/Irish_Athletic_Boxing_Association "Irish Athletic Boxing Association"). Ireland has won more medals in boxing than in any other Olympic sport. [Michael Carruth](/wiki/Michael_Carruth "Michael Carruth") won a gold medal and [Wayne McCullough](/wiki/Wayne_McCullough "Wayne McCullough") won a silver medal in the [Barcelona Olympic Games](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"). In 2008 Kenneth Egan won a silver medal in the Beijing Games.[[203]](#cite_note-207) [Paddy Barnes](/wiki/Paddy_Barnes "Paddy Barnes") secured bronze in those games and gold in the [2010 European Amateur Boxing Championships](/wiki/2010_European_Amateur_Boxing_Championships "2010 European Amateur Boxing Championships") (where Ireland came 2nd in the overall medal table) and [2010 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games"). [Katie Taylor](/wiki/Katie_Taylor "Katie Taylor") has won gold in every European and World championship since 2005. In August 2012 at the Olympic Games in London, Taylor created history by becoming the first Irish woman to win a gold medal in boxing in the 60 kg lightweight.[[204]](#cite_note-208) More recently, Kellie Harrington won a gold medal at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.[[205]](#cite_note-209)

#### Other sports

Main articles: [Horse racing in Ireland](/wiki/Horse_racing_in_Ireland "Horse racing in Ireland"), [Athletics in Ireland](/wiki/Athletics_in_Ireland "Athletics in Ireland"), and [Golf in Ireland](/wiki/Golf_in_Ireland "Golf in Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Irl-Sligo_horse_racing.jpg/250px-Irl-Sligo_horse_racing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irl-Sligo_horse_racing.jpg)

[Horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") in [Sligo](/wiki/Sligo "Sligo")

Horse racing and greyhound racing are both popular in Ireland. There are frequent horse race meetings and greyhound stadiums are well-attended. The island is noted for the breeding and training of race horses and is also a large exporter of racing dogs.[[206]](#cite_note-210) The horse racing sector is largely concentrated in the County Kildare.[[207]](#cite_note-211)

Irish athletics is an all-Ireland sport governed by [Athletics Ireland](/wiki/Athletics_Ireland "Athletics Ireland"). [Sonia O'Sullivan](/wiki/Sonia_O%27Sullivan "Sonia O'Sullivan") won two medals at 5,000 metres on the track; gold at the 1995 [World Championships](/wiki/IAAF_World_Championships_in_Athletics "IAAF World Championships in Athletics") and silver at the [2000 Sydney Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics"). [Gillian O'Sullivan](/wiki/Gillian_O%27Sullivan "Gillian O'Sullivan") won silver in the 20k walk at the 2003 World Championships, while sprint hurdler [Derval O'Rourke](/wiki/Derval_O%27Rourke "Derval O'Rourke") won gold at the 2006 World Indoor Championship in Moscow. Olive Loughnane won a silver medal in the 20k walk at the World Athletics Championships in Berlin in 2009.[[208]](#cite_note-212)

Golf is very popular, and golf tourism is a major industry attracting more than 240,000 golfing visitors annually.[[209]](#cite_note-213) The [2006 Ryder Cup](/wiki/2006_Ryder_Cup "2006 Ryder Cup") was held at [The K Club](/wiki/The_Kildare_Hotel_and_Golf_Club "The Kildare Hotel and Golf Club") in County Kildare.[[210]](#cite_note-214) [Pádraig Harrington](/wiki/P%C3%A1draig_Harrington "Pádraig Harrington") became the first Irishman since [Fred Daly](/wiki/Fred_Daly_(golfer) "Fred Daly (golfer)") in 1947 to win the [British Open](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship") at [Carnoustie](/wiki/Carnoustie "Carnoustie") in July 2007.[[211]](#cite_note-215) He successfully defended his title in July 2008[[212]](#cite_note-216) before going on to win the [PGA Championship](/wiki/PGA_Championship "PGA Championship") in August.[[213]](#cite_note-217) Harrington became the first European to win the PGA Championship in 78 years and was the first winner from Ireland. Three golfers from Northern Ireland have been particularly successful. In 2010, [Graeme McDowell](/wiki/Graeme_McDowell "Graeme McDowell") became the first Irish golfer to win the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)"), and the first European to win that tournament since 1970. [Rory McIlroy](/wiki/Rory_McIlroy "Rory McIlroy"), at the age of 22, won the 2011 U.S. Open, while [Darren Clarke](/wiki/Darren_Clarke "Darren Clarke")'s latest victory was the [2011 Open Championship](/wiki/2011_Open_Championship "2011 Open Championship") at Royal St. George's. In August 2012, McIlroy won his 2nd major championship by winning the USPGA Championship by a record margin of 8 shots.

#### Recreation

The west coast of Ireland, [Lahinch](/wiki/Lahinch "Lahinch") and [Donegal Bay](/wiki/Donegal_Bay "Donegal Bay") in particular, have popular surfing beaches, being fully exposed to the Atlantic Ocean. Donegal Bay is shaped like a funnel and catches west/south-west Atlantic winds, creating good surf, especially in winter. Since just before the year 2010, [Bundoran](/wiki/Bundoran "Bundoran") has hosted European championship surfing. [Scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving") is increasingly popular in Ireland with clear waters and large populations of sea life, particularly along the western seaboard. There are also many shipwrecks along the coast of Ireland, with some of the best [wreck dives](/wiki/Wreck_dives "Wreck dives") being in [Malin Head](/wiki/Malin_Head "Malin Head") and off the County Cork coast.[[214]](#cite_note-218)

With thousands of lakes, over 14,000 kilometres (8,700 mi) of fish-bearing rivers and over 7,500 kilometres (4,660 mi) of [coastline](/wiki/List_of_Irish_counties_by_coastline "List of Irish counties by coastline"), Ireland is a popular [angling](/wiki/Angling "Angling") destination. The temperate Irish climate is suited to sport angling. While [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") and [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout") fishing remain popular with anglers, salmon fishing, in particular, received a boost in 2006 with the closing of the salmon [driftnet](/wiki/Driftnet "Driftnet") fishery. [Coarse fishing](/wiki/Coarse_fishing "Coarse fishing") continues to increase its profile. Sea angling is developed with many beaches mapped and signposted,[[215]](#cite_note-219) and the range of sea angling species is around 80.[[216]](#cite_note-220)

### Food and drink

Main article: [Irish cuisine](/wiki/Irish_cuisine "Irish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Cheese_61_bg_080106.jpg/250px-Cheese_61_bg_080106.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cheese_61_bg_080106.jpg)

[Gubbeen cheese](/wiki/Gubbeen_cheese "Gubbeen cheese"), an example of the resurgence in Irish cheese making

Food and cuisine in Ireland take their influence from the crops grown and animals farmed in the island's temperate climate and from the social and political circumstances of Irish history. For example, whilst from the Middle Ages until the arrival of the potato in the 16th century the dominant feature of the Irish economy was the herding of cattle, the number of cattle a person owned was equated to their social standing.[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221) Thus herders would avoid slaughtering a milk-producing cow.[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221)

For this reason, pork and [white meat](/wiki/White_meat "White meat") were more common than beef, and thick fatty strips of salted [bacon](/wiki/Bacon "Bacon") (known as rashers) and the eating of salted butter (i.e. a dairy product rather than beef itself) have been a central feature of the diet in Ireland since the Middle Ages.[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221) The practice of bleeding cattle and mixing the blood with milk and butter (not unlike the practice of the [Maasai](/wiki/Maasai_people "Maasai people")) was common[[218]](#cite_note-222) and [black pudding](/wiki/Black_pudding "Black pudding"), made from blood, grain (usually barley) and seasoning, remains a breakfast staple in Ireland. All of these influences can be seen today in the phenomenon of the "[breakfast roll](/wiki/Breakfast_roll "Breakfast roll")".

The introduction of the potato in the second half of the 16th century heavily influenced cuisine thereafter. Great poverty encouraged a subsistence approach to food, and by the mid-19th century, the vast majority of the population sufficed with a diet of potatoes and milk.[[219]](#cite_note-223) A typical family, consisting of a man, a woman and four children, would eat 18 stone (110 kg) of potatoes per week.[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221) Consequently, dishes that are considered as national dishes represent a fundamental simplicity to cooking, such as the [Irish stew](/wiki/Irish_stew "Irish stew"), [bacon and cabbage](/wiki/Bacon_and_cabbage "Bacon and cabbage"), [boxty](/wiki/Boxty "Boxty"), a type of potato pancake, or [colcannon](/wiki/Colcannon "Colcannon"), a dish of [mashed potatoes](/wiki/Mashed_potatoes "Mashed potatoes") and [kale](/wiki/Kale "Kale") or [cabbage](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage").[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221)

Since the last quarter of the 20th century, with a re-emergence of wealth in Ireland, a "New Irish Cuisine" based on traditional ingredients incorporating international influences[[220]](#cite_note-224) has emerged.[[221]](#cite_note-225) This cuisine is based on fresh vegetables, fish (especially salmon, trout, [oysters](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster"), [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel") and other shellfish), as well as traditional soda breads and the wide range of hand-made [cheeses](/wiki/List_of_Irish_cheeses "List of Irish cheeses") that are now being produced across the country. An example of this new cuisine is "Dublin Lawyer": lobster cooked in whiskey and cream.[[222]](#cite_note-226) The potato remains however a fundamental feature of this cuisine and the Irish remain the highest per capita[[217]](#cite_note-food_companion-221) consumers of potatoes in Europe. Traditional regional foods can be found throughout the country, for example [coddle](/wiki/Coddle "Coddle") in Dublin or [drisheen](/wiki/Drisheen "Drisheen") in Cork, both a type of sausage, or [blaa](/wiki/Blaa "Blaa"), a doughy white bread particular to Waterford.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Distillerie_OldBushmills.jpg/250px-Distillerie_OldBushmills.jpg)](/wiki/File:Distillerie_OldBushmills.jpg)

The [Old Bushmills Distillery](/wiki/Old_Bushmills_Distillery "Old Bushmills Distillery") in County Antrim

Ireland once dominated the world's market for whiskey, producing 90% of the world's whiskey at the start of the 20th century. However, as a consequence of bootleggers during the [prohibition in the United States](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") (who sold poor-quality whiskey bearing Irish-sounding names thus eroding the pre-prohibition popularity for Irish brands)[[223]](#cite_note-227) and tariffs on Irish whiskey across the British Empire during the [Anglo-Irish Trade War](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Trade_War "Anglo-Irish Trade War") of the 1930s,[[224]](#cite_note-228) sales of Irish whiskey worldwide fell to a mere 2% by the mid-20th century.[[225]](#cite_note-229) In 1953, an Irish government survey, found that 50% of whiskey drinkers in the United States had never heard of [Irish whiskey](/wiki/Irish_whiskey "Irish whiskey").[[226]](#cite_note-230)

Irish whiskey, as researched in 2009 by the [CNBC](/wiki/CNBC "CNBC") American broadcaster, remains popular domestically and has grown in international sales steadily over a few decades.[[227]](#cite_note-not_luck-231) Typically CNBC states Irish whiskey is not as smoky as a [Scotch whisky](/wiki/Scotch_whisky "Scotch whisky"), but not as sweet as [American](/wiki/American_whiskey "American whiskey") or [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_whiskey "Canadian whiskey") whiskies.[[227]](#cite_note-not_luck-231) Whiskey forms the basis of [cream liqueurs](/wiki/Irish_Cream "Irish Cream"), such as [Baileys](/wiki/Baileys_Irish_Cream "Baileys Irish Cream"), and the "[Irish coffee](/wiki/Irish_coffee "Irish coffee")" (a [cocktail](/wiki/Cocktail "Cocktail") of coffee and whiskey reputedly invented at [Foynes flying-boat station](/wiki/Foynes "Foynes")) is probably the best-known Irish cocktail.

[Stout](/wiki/Porter_(beer) "Porter (beer)"), a kind of [porter beer](/wiki/Porter_beer "Porter beer"), particularly [Guinness](/wiki/Guinness "Guinness"), is typically associated with Ireland, although historically it was more closely associated with London. Porter remains very popular, although it has lost sales since the mid-20th century to [lager](/wiki/Lager "Lager"). [Cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider"), particularly *[Magners](/wiki/Magners "Magners")* (marketed in the Republic of Ireland as *Bulmers*), is also a popular drink. [Red lemonade](/wiki/Red_lemonade "Red lemonade"), a soft-drink, is consumed on its own and as a mixer, particularly with whiskey.[[228]](#cite_note-232)

## Economy

Main articles: [Economy of the Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Economy of the Republic of Ireland") and [Economy of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_Northern_Ireland "Economy of Northern Ireland")

See also: [International Financial Services Centre, Dublin](/wiki/International_Financial_Services_Centre,_Dublin "International Financial Services Centre, Dublin")

The GDP of the Republic of Ireland as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ireland&action=edit) was €423.5 billion (nominal),[[229]](#cite_note-IMFWEOIE-233) and in Northern Ireland in 2021, it was £52 billion (GVA Balanced).[[230]](#cite_note-NI_GDP-234) The GDP per capita in the Republic of Ireland was €84,049.9 (nominal) as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ireland&action=edit),[[229]](#cite_note-IMFWEOIE-233) and in Northern Ireland 2021 was £27,154 (GVA Balanced).[[230]](#cite_note-NI_GDP-234) The Republic of Ireland and the United Kingdom measure these numbers differently.

Despite the two jurisdictions using two distinct currencies (the euro and [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")), a growing amount of commercial activity is carried out on an all-Ireland basis. This has been facilitated by the two jurisdictions' former shared membership of the European Union, and there have been calls from members of the business community and policymakers for the creation of an "all-Ireland economy" to take advantage of [economies of scale](/wiki/Economies_of_scale "Economies of scale") and boost competitiveness.[[231]](#cite_note-235)

### Economic history

Main article: [Economic history of Ireland](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Ireland "Economic history of Ireland")

Prior to partition in 1921, Ireland had a long history as an economic colony – first, partially, of the Norse, via their cities (9th to 10th centuries CE), and later, to varying extents, of polities related to England. Though the climate and soil favoured certain forms of agriculture,[[238]](#cite_note-242) [trade barriers](/wiki/Trade_barrier "Trade barrier") frequently hobbled its development. Repeated invasions and plantations disrupted [land-ownership](/wiki/Land_tenure "Land tenure"), and [multiple failed uprisings](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_(disambiguation) "Irish Rebellion (disambiguation)") also contributed to repeated phases of [deportation](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation") and of [emigration](/wiki/Irish_emigrant "Irish emigrant").

Salient events in the economic history of Ireland include:

* 16th and 17th centuries: confiscation and redistribution of land in the [Plantations of Ireland](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland "Plantations of Ireland")
* 1845–1849: [The Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") occasioned depopulation and mass emigration
* 1846: Westminster's repeal of the [Corn Laws](/wiki/Corn_Laws "Corn Laws") disrupted Irish agriculture[[239]](#cite_note-243)

### Major industries

#### Tourism

Main article: [Tourist destinations in Ireland](/wiki/Tourist_destinations_in_Ireland "Tourist destinations in Ireland")

There are two [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") on the island: the *[Brú na Bóinne](/wiki/Br%C3%BA_na_B%C3%B3inne "Brú na Bóinne")* complex and the [Giant's Causeway](/wiki/Giant%27s_Causeway "Giant's Causeway").[[240]](#cite_note-244) Several other places are on the tentative list, for example the Burren, the Ceide Fields[[241]](#cite_note-245) and [Mount Stewart](/wiki/Mount_Stewart "Mount Stewart").[[242]](#cite_note-246)

Some of the most visited sites in Ireland include [Bunratty Castle](/wiki/Bunratty_Castle "Bunratty Castle"), the [Rock of Cashel](/wiki/Rock_of_Cashel "Rock of Cashel"), the [Cliffs of Moher](/wiki/Cliffs_of_Moher "Cliffs of Moher"), [Holy Cross Abbey](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Abbey "Holy Cross Abbey") and [Blarney Castle](/wiki/Blarney_Castle "Blarney Castle").[[243]](#cite_note-facts2006-247) Historically important monastic sites include [Glendalough](/wiki/Glendalough "Glendalough") and [Clonmacnoise](/wiki/Clonmacnoise "Clonmacnoise"), which are maintained as [national monuments](/wiki/National_Monument_(Ireland) "National Monument (Ireland)") in the Republic of Ireland.[[244]](#cite_note-248)

The Dublin region receives the most tourists[[243]](#cite_note-facts2006-247) and is home to several of the most popular attractions such as the [Guinness Storehouse](/wiki/Guinness_Storehouse "Guinness Storehouse") and Book of Kells.[[243]](#cite_note-facts2006-247) The west and south west, which includes the [Lakes of Killarney](/wiki/Lakes_of_Killarney "Lakes of Killarney") and the [Dingle peninsula](/wiki/Dingle_peninsula "Dingle peninsula") in County Kerry and [Connemara](/wiki/Connemara "Connemara") and the [Aran Islands](/wiki/Aran_Islands "Aran Islands") in County Galway, are also popular tourist destinations.[[243]](#cite_note-facts2006-247)

[Stately homes](/wiki/Stately_home "Stately home"), built during the 17th, 18th and 19th centuries in [Palladian](/wiki/Palladian "Palladian"), [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") and [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") styles, such as [Castle Ward](/wiki/Castle_Ward "Castle Ward"), [Castletown House](/wiki/Castletown_House "Castletown House"), [Bantry House](/wiki/Bantry_House "Bantry House"), [Strokestown Park](/wiki/Strokestown_Park "Strokestown Park") and [Glenveagh Castle](/wiki/Glenveagh_Castle "Glenveagh Castle") are also of interest to tourists. Some have been converted into hotels, such as [Ashford Castle](/wiki/Ashford_Castle "Ashford Castle"),[[245]](#cite_note-249) [Castle Leslie](/wiki/Castle_Leslie "Castle Leslie")[[246]](#cite_note-250) and [Dromoland Castle](/wiki/Dromoland_Castle "Dromoland Castle").[[247]](#cite_note-251)

#### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Ireland](/wiki/Energy_in_Ireland "Energy in Ireland")

Although for most of their existence electricity networks in the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland were entirely separate, the island has operated for some time as a single market for electricity.[[248]](#cite_note-252) Both networks were designed and constructed independently post-partition but they are now connected with three interlinks[[249]](#cite_note-253) and are also connected through Great Britain to mainland Europe. The situation in Northern Ireland is complicated by the issue of private companies not supplying [Northern Ireland Electricity](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Electricity "Northern Ireland Electricity") with enough power. In the Republic of Ireland, the [ESB](/wiki/ESB_Group "ESB Group") has failed to modernise its power stations, and the availability of power plants has recently averaged only 66%, one of the worst such rates in Western Europe. [EirGrid](/wiki/EirGrid "EirGrid") has started building a [HVDC](/wiki/High-voltage_direct_current "High-voltage direct current") transmission line between Ireland and Great Britain with a capacity of 500 MW,[[250]](#cite_note-254) about 10% of Ireland's peak demand.

As with electricity, the natural gas distribution network is also now all-island, with a pipeline linking [Gormanston, County Meath](/wiki/Gormanston,_County_Meath "Gormanston, County Meath"), and [Ballyclare](/wiki/Ballyclare "Ballyclare"), County Antrim.[[251]](#cite_note-255) Most of Ireland's gas comes through interconnectors between [Twynholm](/wiki/Twynholm "Twynholm") in Scotland and [Ballylumford](/wiki/Ballylumford_power_station "Ballylumford power station"), County Antrim and [Loughshinny](/wiki/Loughshinny "Loughshinny"), County Dublin. Supplies come from the Corrib Gas Field, off the coast of County Mayo, with a supply previously also coming from the Kinsale gas field off the County Cork coast.[[252]](#cite_note-256)[[253]](#cite_note-257) The County Mayo field faces some localised opposition over [a controversial decision](/wiki/Corrib_gas_controversy "Corrib gas controversy") to refine the gas onshore.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Maam_Cross_turf-cutting_geograph-3178833-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg/250px-Maam_Cross_turf-cutting_geograph-3178833-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maam_Cross_turf-cutting_geograph-3178833-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)

Turf-cutting near [Maam Cross](/wiki/Maam_Cross "Maam Cross") by the road to Leenane, County Galway

Ireland has an ancient industry based on [peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") (known locally as "turf") as a source of energy for home fires. A form of [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") energy, this source of heat is still widely used in rural areas. However, because of the ecological importance of peatlands in storing carbon and their rarity, the EU is attempting to protect this habitat by fining Ireland for digging up peat. In cities, heat is generally supplied by natural gas or [heating oil](/wiki/Heating_oil "Heating oil"), although some urban suppliers distribute sods of turf as "smokeless fuel" for domestic use.

The Republic has a strong commitment to renewable energy and ranks as one of the top 10 markets for [clean-technology](/wiki/Clean_technology "Clean technology") investment in the 2014 Global Green Economy Index.[[254]](#cite_note-ggei-258) Research and development in [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") (such as [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power")) has increased since 2004. Large [wind farms have been constructed](/wiki/List_of_wind_farms_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "List of wind farms in the Republic of Ireland") in Cork, Donegal, Mayo and Antrim. The construction of wind farms has in some cases been delayed by opposition from local communities, some of whom regard the [wind turbines](/wiki/Wind_turbine "Wind turbine") as unsightly. The Republic is hindered by an ageing network that was not designed to handle the varying availability of power that comes from wind farms. The ESB's [Turlough Hill](/wiki/Turlough_Hill "Turlough Hill") facility is the only power-storage facility in the state.[[255]](#cite_note-259)
